text:
MR. MERCHANT:
Has it ever occurred to you that
The Leader goes into the homes
of the best paid workmen of New
ark and Licking county? It is
carefully read by every adult mem
ber of the household.
Vol. IV
STRIKE ORDERED
THE BIG BROTHERHOODS WON’T
OBEY R. R. MANDATES
Two Million Railroad Workers Decide
By Overwhelming Majority to
Resist Wage Cutting and
Abolition of Working
Rules
(By Federated Press)
Chicago—While 2,000,000 organized
railway workers are perfecting their
plans for a strike October 30 against
two cuts in pay amounting to 22 per
cent reduction and against abolition
of working rules prohibiting piece
work and other obnoxious practices
the metropolitan press is loosing its
poison gas propaganda. The propa
ganda aims to bring dissension be
tween the “Big Five” brotherhoods of
train service men and the larger group
of shop workers, maintenance of way
men and other rail workers.
A local paper prints several columns
purporting to be an interview with an
anonymous “chief of one of the eleven
subsidiary unions who said he will
‘fight the strike tooth and nail.’ The
chief is made to say in great detail
just how the heads of the Big Five
“at the eleventh hour refused to give
pledges to back the luckless eleven
unions any longer than may be neces
sary to gain the concessions the
brotherhoods want from the rail
roads.” There follows a disquisition
on the impossibility of winning the
strike—all put in the mouth of the
anonymous railway labor leader.
Strike headquarters have been
opened in this city with Vice-Presi
dent Daugherty of the Brotherhood
of Locomotive Engineers in charge.
The brotherhood chiefs have returned
to their homes but conferences of
leaders of the other unions are held
here this week.
The strike order was sent out Sat
urday evening after the union execu
tives had conferred on the latest
move of the railroad presidents in
asking a second cut in nay. Up to
Wednesday the labor leaders had
hoped to avert a strike. W. G. Lee,
head of the trainmen, had declared
there would be no strike and that the
vote of the membership in favor of
-a strike was merely to strengthen the
hands of the union officials in dealing
with the railroads.
On Saturday, Lee announced that
nothing short of a miracle would pre
vent the strike. He said the order
sent out to the Big Five was final, no
provision being made for a postpone
ment of action. Only a satisfactory
settlement before the date set for the
strike to begin has been provided for
in the instructions sent to the general
chairmen.
Trust’s Hypocrisy
Is Shown By Spy Plan
New York—“So long as the steel
companies persist in the employment
of spies and ‘under-cover men’ to com
bat labor unions, all their professions
of good will toward labor may be dis
counted 100 per cent,” says the New
York World.
“The spy is hired to defend indus
trial autocracy, to betray the inter
ests of the working men and to stir
up the sort of trouble with which it
is easiest to deal.
“According to the supplementary
report of the commission of inquiry
of the interchurch movement, the spy
system, which was an integral part
of the managerial machinery of the
steel corporation at the time of the
strike in 1919, is still functioning, with
no reduction in numbers and no change
of attitude.
“There can be but one explanation
for its continuance. The steel cor
poation wants not men but beasts of
burden as employes.
“Every comparison of hours and
wages among steel workers in the
United States with conditions in the
steel mills abroad tends to prove that
the American workman has been given
a raw deal. If there is ever another
strike in the steel industry the public
will know better where to place the
blame.”
CROOKED RAILROAD EXPOSED
Davenport, Iowa Federal Judge
Wade, has sentenced the general
manager of the Muscatine, Burlington
& Southern railroad to one year in
jail. The railroad official pleaded
guilty to making false entries on car
repair cards. The railroad company
was fined $3,000. Attorneys for the
road say they will carry the case to
the United States supreme court, if
necessary.
Attorneys for the interstate com
merce commission showed that in
1919 the railroad charged $30,000 to
other railroads for car repairs, when
the actual value of these repairs did
not exceed $1,200.
It is estimated that through these
false charges the accused railroad ob
tained nearly $50,000 during the past
six years.
Vofiteering In Shoes Is
The Worst Yet Un
covered
Editor Baine of the Shoe Workers’
Journal “lifts the lid” on shoe prices,
and in explaining why labor refuses
to accept wage reductions in this in
dustry he shows who is profiteering.
Before the war, he says, the labor
cost of a pair of shoes retailing at
3.50 ranged around 60 cents. Later,
his shoe sold as high as $12 and the
labor cost advanced to $1 or 8^ per
cent of the retail price.
“Shoes that retailed at $20 or over
would show a still smaller percentage
of labor cost in proportion to the sell
price at retail,” he says.
“During the period of advance, shoe
prices were made without reason. The
excuse given the consumers was the
high cost of material and labor. One
man who paid $18 for a pah of shoes
was blaming labor for it. He was
asked what he supposed labor received
for making that pair of shoes, and he
replied: “Probably four or five dol
lars.” When told that labor received
less than $1.30 he was astounded.
“For something over a year we have
had a falling market for shoe ma
terials. Prices for shoe materials now
very nearly equal pre-war prices. Yet
manufacturers seem unable to make
shoe prices anywhere near pre-war
levels.
“A manufacturer said he wanted to
make a shoe to sell to dealers at $6,
to be sold at retail at $9, and would
like a reduction in labor cost. The
same shoe used to be sold to the re
tailer at $2.85 and retailed at $4.
Labor receives about 40 cents per
pair more on that shoe.
“Labor was asked to contribute
from its 40 cents to help the manu
facturer sell at $6 what he used to
sell at $2.85, when his market for
raw materials is nearly the same as
before the war.
“There'are other instances of pro
posed reduction in labor cost that have
no better foundation than the one just
related. It is not strange that labor
is not enthusiastic to co-operate for
such ends.
“These policies are among the
causes of the public resentment
against the shoe business that results
in the jeering of a shoe manufacturer
on the floor of the house of represen
tatives at Washington.
“We know there circumstances
somewhat extenuating, such as high
rents, freight and other expenses, but
still it is in order for manufacturers
to introduce a little team work into
their lead pencils.
“Labor declines to be the goat for
the public prejudice to pay the freight
for margins that a few years ago
would have seemed extremely extra
vagant.”
Organized Workers
Fighting Coolie Help
San Jose, Cal.—“The strenuous ef
forts made by the Hawaiian sugar
planters to obtain special privileges
from congress are of grave concern
to the organized workers of Cali
fornia,” says Paul Scharrenberg, sec
retary-treasurer of the California
state federation of labor, in his an
nual report.
“The argument has been advanced
that nothing but the importation of
coolie labor can save the sugar in
dustry of the Hawaiian islands. Well,
if congress yields to the plea of this
infant industry, which, during the
war, extorted from the American
people 25 cents for each pound of
sugar, then there will soon be a gen
eral demand for cheap bonded labor
from every union labor crusher on
the continent of America. And if this
demand is satisfied, how can any fair
minded employer survive in business
unless he, too, adopts the slave labor
policy.
“It must be regretfully reported
that the California Japanese exclusion
league did not respond to the state
federation of labor’s invitation to
join in the protest against the pro
posed importation of bonded coolie
labor for the Hawaiian islands.”
TO CONTROL COAL PRICES
Washington—Government regula
tion of the coal industry again looms
in congress. Senator Kenyon has in
troduced two bills dealing with this
subject. One provides penalties for
profiteering in coal and the other for
a regulatory law based on the theory
that coal is charged with a public use.
Senator Kenyon predicts that in
side of 60 days there will be demand
from many quarters for action by con
gress on the coal situation. He said
the country is facing another serious
fuel situation the coming winter, and
the time to act is now.
There are many who are losing in
terest in the forthcoming disarma
ment conference and believe that its
sessions will be profitless, but Wash
ington hotel keepers are not among
them.
COMMITTEE Of 48
AN OPEN LETTER TO UNEM
PLOYMENT CONFERENCE
Claim Made That No Efforts are Be
ing Made to Deal With Real
Cause of Present Situation
The following Open Letter in rela
tion to the Unemployment Conference,
dated October 11th, has been address
ed by Mr. J. A. Hopkins, Executive
Chairman of the Committee of 48, to
Herbert Hoover, Chairman of the Un
employment Conference:
“I have followed with much inter
est the deliberations of the Unemploy
ment Conference and especially the
recommendations which have so fax1
been published, and it has been a
matter of keen regret to me that up
to the present time the Conference
has apparently made no effort to as
certain or deal with the re cause
foi the existing situation. For unless
we ascertain the cause we cannot hope
to supply a remedy.
“We are living today under a sys
tem of minority control by a banking
syndicate whose knowledge ci states
manship and economics is in inverse
ratio to their ability as financial
prestidigitators and while there may
be contributing causes the funda
mental reason why we have an un
employment problem today can be
traced to this fact.
“A year and a half ago prices and
wages were high. Both were fictiti
ous, but we had comparative prospei
ity. Had we adopted an intelligent
method of readjustment prices and
wages would have gradually come
down to a more normal level, and we
would today be enjoying a sounder
prosperity.
“But the banking fraternity had
passed through a period of unprece
dented profiteering and were reluctant
to adopt any plan that would not pre
serve to them an abnormal ratio of
profit. The appetite for money, like
the taste for blood, once aroused can
not be easily satiated.
^They control credit. Industry is
dependent upon credit. When credit
is reduced the wheels w of industry
slacken. When credit is cut off they
stop. Under the pretence of deflating
currency the banking fraternity cur
tailed credit.
“As a result we are today stagger
ing under a tremendous war budget
which absorbs 93 per cent of our Fed
eral taxes, and are facing an unem
ployment problem the seriousness of
which few people realize.
“The only permanent remedy by
which the cause of our unemployment
problem and the other problems origi
nating in our present system of
finance and administration of govern
ment can be eliminated is to destroy
the system of Special Privilege
through which the banking group se
cures its power.
“But ponding the achievement of
these aims, and in order to meet the
immediate emergency which the Un
employment Conference has been
called upon to consider, the Commit
tee of 48 recommends the following
temporary program:
“That the banking group be re
quired to reverse the credit policy
which it has been pursuing for the
past eighteen months.
“That the banking group be re
quested to forthwith discontinue its
foreign investments and curtail in
sofar as possible its present exploita
tion of foreign industries, empytoying
its surplus wealth to meet our do
mestic requirements
“That the Federal and state gov
ernments immediately appropriate
substantial sums for highways, pub
lic improvements, and other necessary
construction work
“That Congress vote against the
Harding Railroad Bill giving another
half billion dollars to privately owned
railroads
“That the Government further pro
vide for the return of all railload re
pair work from the private equip
ment companies to the railroad shops:
“That the policy of the Fedeial gov
ernment shall be that all employers
pay a living wage, and that the hours
of labor shall be not more thun eight
nor less than six hours pet day.
“It is my hope that this program
will be given the consideration it de
sei-ves by the Conference of which
you are Chairman, and that as a re
sult of your deliberations you will not
only endorse this program but will
urge upon Congress the necessity for
immediate relative thereto.”
DEPENDS WHOSE OX IS GORED
The employer who yells about the
“Tyranny of labor unions,” is the
first one to join a trust or associa
tion that fixes prices or limits the out
put. The lawyer and the physician
refuse to counsel with a man outside
of their profession, because they de
sire to maintain the standard of prices
and the monopoly of their profession,
but these same men grow very indig
nant at 4he effort of labor unions to
try and prevent non-union men from
reducing prices of their occupation.
NEWARK, OHIO, FRIDAY, OCTOBER 21, 1921 No. 50
COMMUNIST
agitator!
What labor Is Doing
Wage Increases and Progreaa Made
Throughout the Country
Leflon Causes Women tor'Lose Jobs
San Francisco—Owing to agitation
by the American Legion, every woman
elevator operator in this city has been
discharged, without reference to her
ability or her need.
Would Bar Unemployed From State
San Francisco—A resolution to bar
unemployed workmen from entering
California was the chief result of the
State unemployment conference just
held here.
Armament Expenses Increasing
London—“We are spending for the
current financial year on the army,
navy and air force, £207,794,000,”
writes E. D. Morel, editor, Foreign
Affairs. “Today we are spending four
times more on armaments than we
were in 1913.”
Protects Unionist
Indianapolis, Ind.—Governor Mc
Cray agrees with trade unionists that
justice for miners in West Virginia
is impossible at the present time. He
has refused to grant a requisition for
the return to West Virginia of David
Robb, organizer of the United Mine
Workers.
Welfare Association to Meet
Cincinnati—The Brotherhood Wel
fare Association, with national office
here, has called its annual convention
to meet at Detroit, Mich., November
29-30 and December 1-2. The problem
of unemployment is to be considered
at the convention.
Wages Below Living Cost
Boston—Although it returned a
finding that a self-supporting woman
in this state requires $13.50 a week to
live, the minimum wage commission
has made an award fixing the minin
mum pay of experienced workers in
the minor lines of the candy industry
at $12 a week.
Jioover Unemployment Will Fail
Washington—If the Hoover unem
ployment conference tries to convince
the American wage worker that he
should accept a cut in his pay in order
to stimulate business, it will fail as
certainly as the labor-conscription
schemes of Senator Wadsworth and
other stand-patters failed in 1918,
says Secretary Morrison of the A.
F. of L.
Big Disarmament Parade
Seattle—Workers of Seattle will
stage a huge disarmament parade on
Armistice Day, acording to plans
drawn by the Central Labor Council
concurring in the A. F. of L. plan for
a series of nation-wide protests
against militarism on the day the
disarmament Conference opens in
Washington, D. C.
It has almost come to pass that no
body has a good word to say for Mr.
Morgan’s Steel Trust but the editors
of lUv daily press.

THE RAILROAD PROFITEER
The men who own the railroads
Are working every scheme
To multiply their profits—
To skim off all the cream.
With rates now out of reason
They’re anxious it appears,
To make a cut in wages—
The railroad profiteers.
It seems to be their purpose
To put the section men
Back to the peon schedule,
The good old “dollar-ten,”
Shopmen, carmen, yardmen, clerks,
Trainmen and engineers,
Are getting too much money
Say the railroad profiteers.
They also raise objection
To the basic eight-hour day:
That ten or twelve is just the thing
These coupon-cutters say.
But they’ll soon be shown where to
head in—
They’re flagged now, it appears
The “slow down” signal’s waving
For the railroad profiteers.
—Thomas H. West.
89,000 Subscribe
To Labor Paper
The Locomotive Engineers have
built up a wonderful organization
through the use of common sense.
They have conducted their affairs on
a strictly business basis, and the loud
mouthed, empty-headed theorist or
nut has never been able to get a fol
lowing. There are other organiza
tions which would be far better off if
they could truthfully say the same
thing.
On October 1st every member of
the Locomotive Engineers—8‘J,000 in
all—subscribed for “Labor,” the week
ly labor paper published in Washing
ton, and in doing this the engineers
again displayed the common sense
that has featured the conduct of their
union.
“Labor” is a newspaper that carries
no advertisements. It was founded
by the railroad crafts and has on its
staff some of the best economic and
industrial writers in the country, and
in its columns may be found a true ex
position of the legislative farce now
being enacted in Washington, as well
as a resume of the industrial situation
throughout the country.
The engineers want to know what
is being done—and they wrant the
truth. Instead of filling themselves
with the stuff handed out by the sub
sidized daily political organs they de
pend upon papers which give the
facts—hence their subscription to
“Labor.”
COLLEGE TO AID LESS ARMS
Princeton, N. J. The students of
more than 100 universites and col
leges in the eastern states have been
invited by the senior council of Prince
ton to send delegates here on October
26 for a conference to devise means
of solidifying the young men of the
nation in a movement for the inter
national limitation of disarmament.
Which can you be likened to in your
organization, a drop of oil that helps
to make things run smoothly, or a
grain of sand that tends to hurt the
gears
MR. WORKINGMAN:
The Leader ia your paper. It
publishes matter pertaining to your
life and welfare. It wants your
support. Your cause would be weak
without a labor paper to champion
it. Support The Leader.

HIM
Genera! Labor Notes
Short Items Gathered For Benefit of
Hasty Readers
Bakers Win After Stiff Fight
Seattle Portland master bakers
have given bakery workers a free
hand in organizing their shops and
have granted conciliation and arbitra
tion rights as the result of a stiff
fight by the Bakers’ Union.
Bricklayers Get Contract
Oklahoma City—A new contract
has just been signed between the
Bricklayers’ Union and the Builders*
Association here and will be in effect
until June 30 of next year. A wage
scale of $10 a day is provided.
Timber Workers Gain
Seattle Timber workers have
awakened to the lumber barons’ com
pany “union,” known as the loyal
legion of loggers and lumbermen, and
are returning to the legitimate inter
national Union of Timber Workers.
Will Solve Unemployment Problem
Waterloo, la.—To give employment,
Waterloo’s business men and city of
ficials have endorsed a plan to obtain
a fund through voluntary public sub
scriptions, the money to be used in
making needed improvements on
streets, parks, etc.
Fur Workers Win
Chicago—Fur workers have defeat
ed an attempt to reduce wages $10
a week. Independent fur manufac
turers were the first to quit the fight.
The others quickly abandoned hope
for the anti-union shop and cheap
wages.
Forty-Four Hour Week in Australia
Sidney, N. S. W. The 44-hour
week is now almost universal through
out the Labor State of New South
Wales. Only in rare ca^es is the 48-,
hour week worked as such, and ia
these cases a special “44-hour week”
court is gradually bringing them un
der the 44-hour week.
Fined For Not Paying Legal Wages
Seattle—Many street work con
tractors here are being haled into
court for paying laborers under the
city scale of $4.50. A city ordinance
demands that all contractors on city
work pay at least the minimum. One
contractor was fined $400 and sen
tenced to 30 days in jail for paying
his laborers $3.50 a day.
Bank Trust Blamed for Unemployment
Minneapolis—Responsibility for un
employment was placed at the door
of the banking trust and lax govern
ment officials who neglected to prose
cute profiteers during the war, and a
course of action mapped out to force
city, state and federal officials to take
immediate steps to provide jobs at a
largely attended meeting of unem
ployed ex-service men held here.
Prosperity built upon the misery of
workers is about all the “best brains”
can think of at the moment.

FARMERS’ COLUMN.
THE HAWTHORN.
From the Rural New Yorker.
Our Double-flowering Hawthorns have
been in fhll bloom during the last half
of the month of May, and a splendid show
they have made, with their white, pink
and purple flowers. The single-flower
ing sorts are very handsome, but last but
a few days; while the double-flowers re
main for two or three weeks. These
tiees which are among the most elegant
of the ornamental class, are far too gen
erally neglected by nurserymen, and it
is not an unusual occurrence to find
large nurseries entiraly destitute of Haw-
thorns. Perhaps the demand tor these
trees will not warrant the keeping of
large or small stocks on hand; if so, then
those who desire to ornament their
grounds are at fault in not planting more,
and creating a demand.
Few trees of so hardy a character as
the Hawthorn make so brilliant a show
when in bloom as the single varieties do
when loaded with fruit in autumn. The
flowers of the double sort resemble small
roses crowded into dense clusters, two
or three inches in deameter, as shown in
the accompanying illustration of a small
branch and flower of the Double Pink
Hawthorn. There are not only many
double varieties, but some of the single
that have brilliant flowers, and are well
worthy of cultivation.
We have seen a few of the double
flowering white and red Hawthorns, in
the old gardens a bout our Eastern cities,
that are twenty to thirty feet high ; but
it requires many years for such specimens
to grow, but as the plants commence
blooming when only two or three years
old from bud or graft, there is really no
time wasted.
Our native stocks are excellent for the
double European sorts, and may be bud
ded with almost as much certainty as
the apple. In some soils and localities
the Hawthorn is attacked by borers ; but
with clean culture about the stems, and
an occasional application of lime or ashes,
these pests will do little injury. We ad
vise those who desire handsome trees,
and have them not, to try a few Double
Hawthorns.
A St. Louis paper states that a countryman
came to that city, the other day,, to get a
divorce from his wife. While walking to the
court-room he espied a huge sea-turtle repos
ing upon its back in front of a restaurant,
and, allured by the sight, stopped to exam
ine it thoroughly. Meanwhile his wife was
at work for herself, and, when finally he hur
ried on to the court-room, he learned, to his
dismay, that the case had been decided
against him, and his wife had obtained a
divorce. He did not care so much about the
loss of his wife, as he had engaged another
in anticipation of getting a divorce himself;
but as the wife had obtained a decree against
him, he was by the law precluded from tak
unto himself another rib until the lapse of
five years. He was almost frantic, as he had
his wedding clothes ready, and was to have
been married the next day. His lawyer made
a motion for a rehearing, and it was set for
argument in two days. But the woman was
again too smart for him, tor she, too, had a
husband in waiting, and as soon as the de-
Cree was announced, she went out and mar
ried her man, without stopping to examine
the anatomy of a turtle, or any other fish.
The Yankee Factory Girls.—ln one of
the* factories in Maine, recently, the pro
prietors reduced the wages, whereupon there
was a general determination to strike ; and
at they were obliged to give a month’s no
tice before quitting work, they had mean
while issued a circular to the world at large
in which is the following interesting para
graph: “We are now working out our no
tice-can turn our hands to most anything—
don’t like to be idle—but determined not to
work for nothing where folks can afford to
pay. Who wants Eelp ? We can make bon
nets, dresses, puddings, pies, knit, roast,
stew, and fry; make butter and cheese :
milk cows and feed chickens, and hoe corn,
sweep out the kitchen, put the parlor to
rights, make beds, split wood, kindle fires,
besides being remaikably fond of babies ; in
fact can do any thing the most accomplished
housewife is capable of doing, not forgetting
the scoldings on Mondays and Saturdays;
for specimens of spirit will refer you to our
overseer. Speak quick. Black eyes, fair
foreheads, clustering locks, beautiful as
Hebe; can sing like a seraph, and smile most
bewitchingly; any elderly gentleman in
want of a good housekeeper, or a nice young
man in want of a wife—willing to sustain
either character; in fact we are in the mar
ket. Who bids ? Going, going, gone I Who’s
the lucky man ?”

A St. Louis gentleman who employs a
coolie as a domestic servant, finds that the
paganism of the Chinamen is his greatest
fault. The other morning, the family, in
cluding the cooH Q , were gathered into the
sitting room for prayers. When the exercises
were over it was ascertained that the coolie
had been worshipping an old-fashioned brass
andiron n the fireplace. He mistook it for
an idol, and had offered it two dead rats. He
spends four hours a day in front of that and
iron, saying his catechism, and the impres
sion throughout the neighborhood is that if
anything can be done with a brass andiron
in the way of securing the Chinese idea of a
felicitous hereafter that persistent coolie will
doit.
In Titusville, when a man owns four or
five hundred wells he is generally considered
well off, and “barrelsful of oil” is the
synonym of “pockets full of rocks.” It is
customary to speak of the fashionable young
lady as wearing an eighty-six barrel opera
suit, and when a boy wants to have his teeth
plugged, gets a requistion on the “old man”
for a couple barrels of oil. The system be
ing abundantly lubricated, works very
smoothly.
The tender hearted Indian maiden has
started on her travels again. She was the
daughter of Spotted Bear, one of the biggest
of the big Indians of the Sioux. At Fort
Laramie she fell in love with a pale-face lieu
tenant, and as the young man did not hanker
for a wife of that style, her father sent her
away. Then she began to pine, called the
old fellow to her, made him promise never
to scalp another white man, and died. They
top off’ the story with an assurance that the
old man kept his promise; and that is so im
probable as to spoil all the rest.
A handsome Jewess In New York com
plains that her husband deserted her four
weeks after marriage. He pleaded in excuse
that he had only known her twenty-four
hours when she insisted upon marriage, and
bought him a pair of boots and pantaloons,
to do it in. He resisted as long as possible,
but was captured at last, and it was only on
the fourth week that he found opportunity
to run away. She don’t care anything about
him. but she wants the boots and trousers
back, and the five dollars she gave him to
pay the minister.
NEW DEPARTURE OF COLLEGES.
- From the Prairie Farmer.
As a proof that change and progress are
appearing in those time-honored institu
tions, American colleges, we may men
tion the fact, reported by the Eastern pa
pers, that a member of the graduating
class at Cornell University, at its late
commencement, read an essay on the Pre
vention of Freshets in the Chemung River.
Ten years ago the reading of a paper on
such a commonplace subject would have
been regarded as an outrage on the sanc
tity of commencement day. At that
time young collegians were presumed to
be unacquainted with the names of any
rivers except the Po and Tiber. If they
lad any other knowledge of geography,
it was incidentally learned while reading
the works of Livy, Caesar, Herodotus and
Xenophon. The names of the graduates
appeared on the order of exercises, print
ed in Latin, and the degrees were confer
red in the same language. The exercises
of commencement day commenced with a
Latin salutatory and closed with a Greek
valedictory. The intermediate orations
were on the literature of the days of Au
gustus and the philosophy of the school
of Plato. At that time little that was
practical, little that pertained to the
wants of life and progress, was to be found
in the curriculum of instruction, Greek,
Latin and pure mathematics were the es
sential studies in the college course. The
, trustees of this venerable depositions of
the knowledge of the ancients were most
antiquated clergymen, who kept vigilant
watch that nothing profane or common
should creep into the course of instruc
tion. In an extensive faculty there was
often but one professor of sciences, and
one of modern languages ; and to the last
mentioned was ordinarily given the du
ties of teaching rhetoric and English lit
erature. One lesson a day during a single
term embraced all the instruction given
in chemistry, while the other sciences re
ceived even less attention. Young men
were sent to college to be disciplined, and
the means by which this was to be effect
ed was the study of the classics. That
the acquisition of useful knowledge
would serve a purpose in producing men
tal discipline was not thought of.
Fortunately, a better day has come.
Practical men like Cornell, Lawrence
and Bussey, by their endowments in the
aid of practical and scientific departments
of colleges, have afforded means for
young men to obtain an education that
will help them in the great struggle of
life. Until very recently the colleges of
the United States seemed designed only
to prepare young men to enter schools
for teaching the studies needed in the so
called learned professions. In the West,
the University of Michigan was the first
to inaugurate courses of instruction for
the complete teaching of the practical
sciences. Its success has been almost
without a parallel. By degrees changes
have been introduced into other colleges.
At present, Harvard University, the old
est college in America, maintains nine
distinct schools of instruction aside from
the regular college course, which may be
regarded as a very irregular course, in
asmuch as students are permitted at the
end of the Freshman year to to choose
the studies they are to pursue.
In truth, almost all the great colleges
in the country are now introducing
changes in their course of instruction.
Yale, Columbia, Brown, have elective
courses ; while in many other colleges the
students are at liberty to substitute mod
ern languages and the sciences for Greek,
Latin and pure mathematics during a por
tion of the time they are in college. The
present year is witnessing a wide-spread
change in the management of colleges.
The greatly increased number of students
of Harvard, since the change- was made,
has shown other colleges the advantage
of making similar changes. We are glad
too, to see that the plan of allowing the
graduates of colleges to elect the trustees
of their Alma Mater is being regarded
with favor, as it has a tendency to put
the management of these institutions
into the hands of young and progressive
men.
The credit of organizing a college in
this country on the principle of allowing
the students to select the studies they are
to pursue, is due to Thomas Jefferson,
and it was first put into operation in the
University of Virginia. He, too, it was
who proposed to treat college students as
men, and not as boys. In the University
he founded, and which was long the
pride of the south, there was no marking
for recitations, no fines for absence from
chapel exercises, no conduct rules, such
as are often posted up in juvenile school
rooms. Tne students are presumed to be
gentlemen, and to understand the obliga
tions they are under to society and to
their teachers. This college confers no
honorary degrees whatever ; but it per
mits any person after attending lectures
one year to present himself for examina
tion for any degree which the college
confers, the candidate pledging himself
that he has “neither given or received
assistance.”
Happy Coincidences. —What a num
ber of happy historical coincidences the
universal Peace Treaty of Washington
has given birth to. First, the British
Commissioners arrived here on that
greatest birthday anniversary that Amer
ica celebrates—the 22d of February ; the
treaty was completed and signed on the
24th of May, the birthday anniversary
most acceptable to Englishmen—that of
Queen Victoria; the ratifications were
exchanged in London on the 17th of June
—the anniversary of the battle of Bunk
er Hill, the opening fight of the American
Revolution, and a fight in which both
sides gained equal glory, for while the
British unmistakably whipped us, we
gained all the glory ; and lastly, the
treaty was proclaimed as the accepted
law of the land and of the two nations
on the glorious Fourth of July. Did any
greater conglomeratton of auspicious cir
cumstances ever cluster about so great a
masterpiece of statesmanship ? Does not
every date certify to the wise provisions
of the treaty for maintaining peace and
good will among sll English-speaking
people?—X Y. Herald.

Thero may be a buying strike but

J
of notice in itself And the whole Idea

esting game than ever it was before

Two French towns displayed recent

lino long nt Icakt so the cable reports

nnd extremely effective wny of using
trimming on tho eer popular gown of
all blnck
The Premet model Is one of those

Interesting new turn falling to the

or topcoats This Is a very smart way

larly good looking Of course the cus

hne been trcnted thus far but It Is

sort is something nrtlstlc and worthy the coat
Sport Suits on Same Principle

ent leather hnt for the outfit and a
white velours skirt Anil thn whnln fa
Just ns effective as It con be forming
u distinct relief from the all too se
vere sport attire to which we hnve

I have been discovering the coun
terparts of these Paris pretties.
If you are planning an early vacation trip or cruise, be sure to have
a coat ol this type on hand for comfort and smartness. ’J his swagger
model is erwheted of knitting worsted in the favorite Afghan stitch,
which is respcnsibl* lor its different appearance. Olivia de Haviland,
i . delighted with this hand-crocheted coat, and finds many occasions
for wearing it.
Swagger Model
Practical Designs
I breathed a sigh of relief when
I carefully examined all these new
pleated affairs, and discovered that
good common sense had been used
by the designers, to see that the
I pleats were so handled that they
would really stay in. In skirts
they are stitched to a point above
the knees. This not only, preserves
them, but gives a nice, fitted hip
line.
Washable Skirts
If you like to go striding over
the golf course, or at
tennis, pleated skirts are going to
prove your lucky fashion. Pleated
all around for lots ot leg room.
Their stitching, which is most im
portant. as I've said before, is re
leased just above the knee.
RATIO FOR 4 ACES FIGURED
TOLEDO. (U.R) The probability
of the bridge player receiving a
hand with four aces but no kings,
queens or jacks, is 922.393,263.252,-
800 divided by 2.535,338,006.460.608.-
000—take it from Dr. John B.
Brandberry, professor of mathema
tics in the University of Toledo.
IMPERIAL VALLEY PRESS, EL CENTRO, CALIF.
ORIGINALITY
MARKS ALL
NEW HATS
Our thoughts naturally turn to
hats in the springtime. Somehow
a new bonnet seems to be as much
a part of that seasen as the flow
ers that bloom. And incidentally
tliis year all the flowers that
bloom aren't in the fields. At least
more than half of them are on
ome lovely hat.
It is really very difficult to put
your finger on any definite trend
in millinery this season for the
secret of success in the bonnet
world seems to be originality. But
we do find that a large group of
them seem to have been inspired
bv censant st vies.
Wide Brims
There are some hats inspired
by the Flemish and yet others by
Bretons and many more by the
Chinese. Some have high crowns
and some are shallow, some have
wide brims and others none at all.
You can choose whatever looks
most becoming to your type.
One of the nicest is a little bon
net of Tuscan weave straw. It's
really just a brim with an open
crown. A strip of navy grosgrain
ribbon goes over the top of the
head to keep it on and perched on
this is a large bunch of poppy
buds. It has all the charm of the
open countryside done in the man
ner cf a Paris salon.
Coolie Type
Another for the coming season
is a coolie hat of rough straw in
a grand shade of adobe red. The
under part of the brim and the
tight little headpiece are faced
with a coarse veil of braid in navy
blue. It extends beyond the brim
making a flattering frame for the
face.
R ugh straw again, this time in
natural color, is the fabric of an
other Spring bonnet. It has a
pointed round crown, and a wide
rolled-up brim edged with a
flange of dubonnet silk that is dot
ted with large white coin dots. In
the front are perched two ragged
flowers, one of the red fabric and
the other of green.
Several Shades of
Blue Feature New
Stylish Garments
In the class cf the old favorite,
blue, we find one called Canter
bury blue, Parma blue and a new
way of expressing navy blue,
namely, battalion blue, a swagger
military tone.
In the copper or rust shades
is chaudron. which is the French
name for a copper kettle, an im
portant color not only in fabrics
but also in accessories. Then
there's safra. capucine. caramel
brown and toastbeige.
Greens we find with such in
triguing names as amandine, res
eda green, pottery green, chalk
green, Tyrol and olivite. Running
from pink to red we find rose
raspberry, duskrose, cherrywood,
ruby red and cerise.
In the grays is a lovely light
shade called dovetone and anoth
er called Norfolk gray.
Among the pastels are sun
dawn (a bright tyellow), azure
blue, rosecloud and coralite. Shell
bisque is a pinkish beige. Don't
say you can't suit yourself in col
ors this year!
Shoulders Rate High
When You re Talking
About New Sleeves
By MILLICENT
Readers, we give you Madame
Schiaparelli, the iady who likes to
design architecturally. She knows
that shoulders squared, make waist-
Squared shoulders, short leg
c'mutton sleeves, are a gay tea
time fashion for Spring, 1936.
lines and hips more .slender. It is
her favorite trick this Spring, and
by the way, mine . . . and I'm here
to predict that it will soon be
yours.
In tailored suits you expect your
shoulders to be .squared . . . but
when you encounter the style in
dresses, it becomes an unexpected
bit ol styling that you are going
to take to like we look to "The
Music Goes ’Round and ’Round’’!
Little Leg o'Mutton
I am always a softie for any
fashion that harks of period styles.
That’s why the little leg o'mutton
ha., me gaping into shop windows.
It is full at the top, gradually nar
rowing down to just below the el
bow’ where it ends abruptly and
charmingly without a semblance of
a cuff. I like this sleeve best for
Spring and Spring only, because
I am afraid that the below-elbow
tightness would not be the mo t
comfortable fashion for Summer.
The Bell Sleeve
If you bought a dress with this
sleeve last season, you were just
a little bit ahead of fashion, for
they are right up in front in smart
ness now. Trace their ancestry
back to the Chinese, for they are
straight and full, hanging free
from shoulder to the point of ter
mination which may be anywhere
from elbow to wrist. I like this
sleeve far better than the ordin
ary puff sleeve, and there isn't a
single, solitary woman who can
not wear it becomingly. Because it
is not caught tight at any point, it
will be comfortable for wear all
Summer long.
Sporty Coats
Jean Muir, of Hollywood is showing the newest idea in sport swag
ger coats featuring lace cords on the tuxedo reverse as well as on
the cuffs. Ihe reglan shoulder and patch pockets are added sporty
touches. Standard sports yarn is used to knit this model and the
laces are crocheted with shetland floss.
GUNSMITH KEEPS SECRET
OMRO, Wis. <U.R) William Sta
tege, 56-year-old popular Omro
gunsmith, constantly refuses to
divulge his secret formula for
making guns. "Most of my meth
ods are fashioned aftt r those used
in the making of the gun used by
Daniel Boone." tie says.

ALAMEDA, Cal. (U.R) A parent
survey showed a ten to one vote
in favor of bicycling on sidewalks
instead of streets. Police records
showed only two minor accidents
from sidewalk bicycling in recent
years as against several deaths
from street accidents.
WEDNESDAY, FEBRUARY 26,
CHINESE INFLUENCE DOMINATES
COLORS OF MILADY’S WARDROBE
Chinese influence for spring
comes mostly in color and in the
styles cf street and afternoon
dresses. But of course the Chinese
lounging pajamas, for intimate
dinner parties and cocktail affairs
for your closest friends, always
are in style.
Chinese colors include strange
blue-greens and golds, turning from
orange to vermillion. You'll see
many of these colors in the new
street costumes, with tunic coats
and mandarin sleeves. Very high
1936 GLOVES
ADD TOUCH
OF GAIETY
While the new evening gowns
are coming in the filmiest fabrics,
such as mousseline de sole, organ
za, tulle and stiffened voile, in
colors as fresh as the first blos
soms of spring, there's a touch
of modern 1936 sophistication ; n
the vivid-colcred gloves worn with
these gowns.
For many flesh-colored net or
lace gowns, you’ll see vivid Ameri
can beauty suede gloves in the
long sixteen-button style. Over
these gloves will be worn bracelets,
with blue saphires and diamonds,
while a diamond pendant set in
saphires will hang over the front
of the dress. American Beauty
velvet crepe in floor-length pat
tern will be an evening wrap fur
the costume., •
Many of the yellow and geld
shades will be high style notes for
the spring evening season. You'll
see organdie and lace in bouffant
robes de style which will be set off
by purple suede gloves, with brace

and gold motif will be carried out
by a wreath of purple flowers in
the hair or by a circular lei of

White in all the sheer fabric
will be very popular for spring.
With a filmy white pleated chif

with bright red or green suede
gloves to match. Pearls as acces

text:
Traill Of Emerald Buddha Leads To Angkor Wat
Fresh Dangers Beset Venturesome
Crew In Shadow Of Long Sought Goal
THE Captain of the" Mary Ann"
tells the tale of how his mate,
Bill Corkey, meets a Ternate danc
ing girl who gives him a map re
vealing the hiding place of the
fabulous Emerald Buddha in Ang
kor Wat, a carved idol sought by
the pirate. Sin Kew. They escape
from Sin Kew’s men to sail for
Saigon. Word of their arrival has
come by fast junk, and a French
man meets them at the dock.
They visit Huii Doon, money
lender, who owes his life to the Cap
tain, and who provides them with
a pack horse, fresh clothes, food
and rifle and a coolie guide to
take them through the jungle
northward toward Angkor Wat.
The money lender also gives them
a message for Li, a Buddhist
priest living near the ruined
Khmer temples. The little party
struggles through the jungle,
resting by day, traveling at night.
A tiger scents them, and follows
their trail. Bill tries to kill it, but
the beast is too wary. They reach
a rock grotto, and enter by torch
light to find Li, the "bonze,’’
standing in a high-ceilinged room
before a towering Buddha.
By Whit Wellman
Chapter 6.
I THOUGHT then Gim’s nody
would stop trembling. The
strange cat accepted us, and
there wasn't danger of bein’
chewed up Must have been the
usual thing, for Li never bothered
to explain.
But Huii Gim wasn’t lookin’ at
the tiger. He kept starin’ hard
at Li, waitin’ for the borne to
speak—afraid, maybe, of what
he'd tell us. I figured we’d learn
several things we hadn’t an
swered.
"I see,” Li began softly, 'you
are walking in a mist. There are
things to know before you travel
further.”
Huii Gim kept trembling a
little shudder starting at hi.t head
an’ working down to his toes Bill
Corkey looks grim an' expectant,
an’ I waited, keeping one eye on
the tiger and one on Li, while the
incense blows toward us.
Probably the bouse hadn’t
talked for a long time. He spoke
slow an’ gentle, like he was teach
in’ school kids a lesson. His voice
soothed the striped cat. which lay
down an’ took a nap.
"We’re walking out of here, Li
—if you don't start," Bill said.
“Later,” Li answered. "There
is time —”
“We’re in a mrry," Bill in
sisted. “Tell us and we ll be mov
ing."
Li’s deep-set eyes gazed down
at Huii Gim, kindly an' quiet.
"Huii Doon’s granddaughter is
The Crumbling Walls of
Angkor Wat Were All Bu
Hidden By the Dense
Tungle Growth.
a good jungle guide,” he said
"You were fortunate to have hei
with you."
Gim’s hand crept into mine and
held tight.
"A girl!” I chuckled. "But
why—”
M i/NEW something’ was funny
Captain. She was scared
we’d find out, so she didn’t talk
a lot. Smart kid." She had Bill’s
approval. Now that I looked at
her, I could see it myself; deli
cate features, she had, not like a
coolie’s.
"Forgive me, please—” she be
gan, but Li shut her up.
“They should ask forgiveness
for being blind.”
“I ask it,” Bill said, grinnin’ at
the joke on himself. He didn't
often overlook a pretty girl.
“Huii Doon is your friend. That
was a trouble to him, he was your
friend; he owed a debt to you,
and must pay it. You were of
service to him at an undignified
time in Borneo. He was faced
with problems. One, before he
knew who you were, to prevent
you securing the Emerald
Buddha. The other, to make you
safe from attack in the
jungle—’’
"Safe!” I stood up and faced
aim. "Do you know what hap
pened in Ternate, on the Mary
Ann, and in Saigon? Every time
we turned around there was
knives an' elephants after us!"
Li smiled, quiet an’ undisturbed.
"He gave you a guide, rifle, a
horse and food for the journey?”
“He did that Why, T don’t
know."
"The whole damned thing’s
crazy," Bill remarked.
"Aye, mad an’ impossible,” I
agreed
HIT IS not needful that you
I know more of the truth,” Li
said softly. His voice grew lower,
seemed to come from a distance,
somewhere behind the shadowed
Buddha. "Except for one thing. Do
you know what lies in Angkor
Wat?"
Bill was getting restless. Sitting
down so long was unnatural and
I knew he was ready to stop lis
tening. There were things we
didn’t understand, and I saw it
wasn’t doing any good to waste
time trying to learn what the
bonze meant.
"You know so much,” Bill said
disrespectfully. “It’s the Emerald
Buddha we're after an' going to
get.”
"No," Li corrected mm A
carvcn figure, yes. the greatest
emerald known. Yet not a Buddha,
not any holy form that man has
worshipped for Ihouranns of
years. You know of the Lost Con
tinent, the sunken island of Mu?"
“I don't." Bill admitted. “What
of it?"
Back in my memory I’d heard
of it once in India, the tale of a
vast island continent in the Pacific
that suffered earthquake and
storm till it sank beneath the
water.
“Ten thousand years ago Mu
went down," Li continued “It
sank almost without trace. One
relic was preserved by a colony
which settled in India. The emer
ald you seek is——"
“Well, what?” Bill demanded.
"The goddess of Mu. It came
trom Tibet, a gift to the Khmer
emperor who knew its worth He
gave his life to keep it from bar
barian hands when Angkor fell
Enemies surrounded the temple
The emperor caused himself to be
sealed in a secret chamber, that
he might protect the goddess
forever. You are among the for
tunate of the world —to know the
truth. For all lurvivors of Mu
have become spirits, and few hu
mans know of the relic of man's
first civilization.”
n% a #HY tell us that, Li?” Bill
yy Corkey got to his feet.
“Fate is sometimes curious, my
impetuous friend. Perhaps my
telling may stop you from seeking
it. We priests cannot use force to
make you go back, yet we are
content to let the emerald god
dess rest in Angkor.”
This was Bill’s party; he was
the one set on finding the treas
ure, so I didn’t speak. Too, I’d a
funny hunch that Li knew what
he was doing.
"It won't rest much longer,"
Bill said. “We’re on our way.”
The young bonze bowed slightly,
his yellow robe falling into folds
around him. The thick top layer
of air in that dim cavern seemed
to descend until it shadowed the
tall figure of the priest. Most of it
was my imagination, but it did
grow darker. You could see the
fat wax candles burning brightly,
but through a haze.
“I ask protection for you anil
the emerald goddess," Li said. “I
seem to read Huii Gim's thoughts,
and see that she wishes to accom
pany you. This, too, may be
vise "
"I do wish so," the girl said
quietly. “If you please.”
Everything was strange about
Li, and the place we were in. 1
looked up, but couldn’t see the
Buddha’s placid face, hidden high
in a blue dusk.
“Li," I said, turning to go
'there’s things I don’t understand
but never mind. You keep the yel
low cat here until w.e’re out again.”
I thought Li nodded, but wasn't
certain. His hand stroked the tig
er’s twitching yellow ear, and
Stone Carvings on Walls of
Angkor Wat.
that’s the last we saw of him unti!
later
OUTSIDE the hairless horse
waited, standing alone un
der the snake's stone head. He’d
been fed, and his belly was big.
"Girl,” Bill asked, "what's this
all about ? Why did you trail along
—and why keep on to Angkor?"
He started leading the horse, with
Gim beside him.
“Please to understand, but 1
like you. Also, I like the Capt'a,”
she added as an afterthought.
“I get that, all "ight.” Bill
agreed, not underestimating his
charm.
"Without me there would be
much trouble,” she explained.
“There'H be plenty with you
along ”
I suggested to him that she’d
been a help on the jungle trail;
kept us on the right path, any
way; and our adventure hadn’t
ended.
"Did it strike you we’d meet
Sip Kew up farther, Captain ? Do
you figure he’s quit us cold and
don't care about the green god?”
"Goddess, Bill. Li told you that.
Don’t you feel brave, goin’ into
danger for another woman?”
I guess Huii Gim didn’t like my
numor
"Another woman ?" she mur
mered
"Captain—shut up, can you?"
Bill walked ahead with the girl
and horse, and I didn’t remind
him what he knew air ady that
the Ternate dancing girl would be
near Angkor, waiting for him.
NO ONE had said Bill’s prin
cess would be at Angkor, and
I didn’t see then how she could
get there before we did. But funny
things happen in that country;
you get to expect the unexpected.
I had a hunch she’d meet us, just
because Sin Kew’s pirates should
have knifed her 'way back in
Ternate No woman’s going to
give up hope of riches because
she likes a man’s face; even Bill
wasn’t crazy enough to think that.
Or, maybe he was. If he thought
about her at all, which I doubted.
Bill was the easy, laughing sort
—no woman could make him mad,
or sad. He just grinned and kept
on goin', like a wise lad should.
If he figured the princess had for
gotten about him. he was plain
foolish She’d use him, right
enough, to get the idol, hut it was
up to Bill to keep it.
“Where.” asked Bill, plodding
along with the pack horse, "is this
Tonle Sap Lake we pass’’”
"Very soon we arrive,” Huii Gim
said “We must go around the
lake, not across. To be seen is
dangerous."
"Huh! You think Sin Kew don’t
know we're cornin’?"
The giri laughed at him Oh.
yes. He knows of us. Yet .ve must
not be foolish and expose our
selves. We shall be silent and eau
tious."
"If you say so,” Bill agreed,
"but it won't make any difference
in the end We're bound to meet
up with his gang of cut-throats.”
“You are a foolish boy,” she
said, looking shy an’ happy.
We hadn’t beaten her for foo'-
ing us, and let her come along
after the trick she’d played. I will
say she made a better-looking girl
than coolie guide, smiling at Bill
an’ tryin’ to keep up. He moved
along fast, now, impatient to get
to the ruins and have things set
tled.
I WAS fed up with jungle my
self, an’ didn’t blame him for
wanting it over. Fightin' storms
an’ weather was different from
scraps on land with a Chinese you
couldn’t locate. It hadn't been
easy, cornin’ up from Saigon.
Worst of it was, we’d expected
trouble an’ hadn’t found it. It's
harder waiting for things to hap
pen than to plunge into action.
You get tense an’ restless, sort of
tied up in a knot, wishin’ for
somethin’ more than shadows to
tangle with—men’s faces to strike
at, bodies to heave out of the way.
No, that jungle hadn’t been good
for us—too calm, an’ peaceful.
Gave you a dead feelin' inside that
came to life with jerks when a
tree monkey screamed. Our nerves
vere like that, all tight.
"Foolish, eh?” Bill grinned.
"We’ve two 45’s, a rifle, an’ that
paranci you brought to cut lianas.
It can cut other things, too, my
girl. You never saw Bill Corkey
in action, have you?"
Huii Gim shuddered and was
silent awhile.
Took us two days to go 'round
Tonle Sap an' aim for Angkor’s
temples through more jungle. We
got so tired, it all seemed like a
dream you have, expectin' to wake
up in time for breakfast. The girl
slowed first, an' we rested every
mile or so. All the excitement
and adventure dropped away,
an' it got to be just a job
to finish before we went to sleep
for a week Huii Gim didn’t let us
forget to keep watch, specially
when Bill and I could have dropped
oh in a doze when we lay down
to let the legs relax.
WE'D stagger to our feet
again, and sort of fall for
'ward. When you're weary, you
can do that. Let the body lean
ahead no’ f-et will set down under
it. Habits are ha:d to change, an’
we'd the habit of marchin' no mat
ter what the legs said to us, which
was plenty
I'd lo3t count of days, but when
the sun came up strong one morn
ing, I suspected we’d come to the
place.
, hen Bill saw it.
"Sacred Buddhas!” he cried
hoaisely. “D'you ever see any
thing like that ?”
I wasn’t tired any more.
Ahead rose the great temple of
Angko Wat dull brown an' red
in the early sun. Green jungle
came close to it, touchin’ the edge

of walls an' towers. But the ruins
had fought back. Aye, they’d come
near losin’ out. as you could see
by some carved stones caught by
lianas an' dragged away, balien?
No, seized by the creeping evil
jungle itself, pulled away inch by
inch from where the Khmers set
them
"It is beauty and death.” said
the girl.
For once Bill didn't have a word
to offer. He stood quiet, surprised
for once in his life. His mouth was
open a little, an’ I wondered why
all the buzzing flies didn't swarm
into it.

text:
ISSUE STUDIED
BY ARB
FEDERAL ADJUSTMENT BOARD
HOLDS PRELIMINARY MEET
ING IN SAN FRANCISCO.
DECREE WILL BE RETROACTIVE.
Workers in Pacific Coast Cities Have
Left Settlement of Their Demands
for Higher Wages to Government
Mediators.
—
SAN FRANCISCO, Oct. 25.—The
federal shipbuilding adjustment board
held its preliminary session here to- i
day to undertake a permanent settle
ment of the wage controversy which
threatened a suspension of govern
ment shipbuilding and other war
emergency work on the Pacific coast.
It announced that any wage agree
ment reached by it would be retroact
ive in the case of Seattle, Portland
and San Francisco workers.
In all these cities striking ship
yard laborers returned to work, leav
ing the settlement of their demands
for higher wages to the federal ship
building adjustment board.
WOMEN OPERATORS DEMAND
HIGHER WAGES AND RECOG
NITION OF THEIR UNION.
SAN FRANCISCO, Oct. 27. — The
ultimatum of union telephone operat
ors for higher wages and recognition
of their union in Washington, Oregon
and California expired without a word
from the Pacific Telephone and Tele
graph company today and a general
coastwise strike at midnight tonight
seems inevitable.
hour women delegates representing
the operators' unions met with the of
ficials of the telephone company here
today in a futile effort to settle all dif
ferences.
Men electrical workers say that they
will strike unless the women operators’
unions are recognized by the company.
KEEP A CARD INDEX.
There should be some kind of a ,
card index kept on the actions of all
men during the war. The names of
all those who try to make an exorbi
tant profit during the war out of the
necessities of life, as well as those
who in any way delay the prepara
tions for war, should be put in the
-box, for there is where every
patriotic person will look for their
names after the war is over. Their
common street names will be on the
end of every man’s tongue when j
peace is gained, and in order that no
thing they do or say shall be forgot
ten when the time comes to attend
to their case, the card index should
be closely kept. After the war is
over and the victory won, many of
them will be around teling what won- j
derful patriots they were and how i
they helped in the good work. That1
class always wants to sit at the head
of the banquet table, when the lights
burn brightly.
_ I
MR. HOOVER AND MUSH.
As Herbert C. Hoover is an able \
and educated man, it is not quite
clear why he persists in talking to
Americans about the necessity of sav
ing the flour for Europeans and eat
ing corn meal at home, because Eur
opeans do not find corn meal as pal
atable as flour, “not understanding
its preparation.”
If Europeans cannot take a printed
recipe for mush, published in their
own language, and make mush, how
on earth can they make war? Mush
is much more easily made, and is far
more delightful than any feature of
rightly-made war save a bloodless
victory.—Louisville Courier.
While trying to light an oil burner
in the boiler of the Bourbon power
plant recently two men, Cliff Allyn
and Dick Lisson, two inhabitants of
Nome, were badly burned.
A SCANDINAVIAN LIBERTY
BELL RINGS OUT
There is much talk in many of the
newspapers about the leaning of the
Swedish people toward the kaiser.
However this may be Old John Lind,
ex-governor of Minnesota, rings his
bell clear, for pure democracy. In a
recent address to his fellow Ameri
cans of Swedish descent he ex
claimed
“As a citizen of Swedish blood I
should deplore trouble between our
nation and Sweden, but you men in
this audience of Swedish blood and
descent must remember that we are
not of the Swedish household. What
ever kindly feelings we may have
toward the Swedish people, we say
to them unless they choose to have a
government in harmony with the as
pirations of the free peoples of the
earth they must suffer the conse
quences of the actions of their rulers.
“As to us Americans of Swedish
blood or descent, they are our friends j
in peace; aye more than friends, for
they are kindred. But in war, if such
should come—God forbid—they are
enemies.”
That is the kind of a ring that ev
ery American Swede’s bell should
have these times, whether he is a la
borer in the woods or on the rail
road. The Swedes in America who
carry the Lind banner will not try to
delay the government in its prepara
tion to defeat the enemy of all man
kind. A Swede that chooses Bill Hay
wood for a leader rather than John
Lind, is a very undesirable citizen
just at this time and should be
guarded with suspicion.
MUST HAVE LABOR.
The Canadian resolutions to im
port Chinese to replace the working
men who may be conscripted is de
scribed as merely a war-time meas
ure.
It will probably be carried out. It
has been carried out elsewhere. The
coolie laborer is so docile and so
cheap that it is a fact that he is ir
resistible.
It is true that he has not the
productive capacity of the white man.
But he can be increased in numbers
with ease and numbers will do what
the individual might not do.
In several places capital has
brought about a well-nigh ideal con
dition by this coolie system. Espe
cially has it been perfected in some
British colonies under a system of
indentures. There the owner of a
plantation and property becomes
monarch of all he surveys and the
laborer is bound to him.
The coolie produces wealth for the
wage that the white man would not
look at unless fresh from the bread
line and thus rendered super-docile.
Nor has it been an unknown condi
tion in the past. Serfdom arose as
such from the slave system of the
latifundists in the Roman empire.
The Athenian democracy was found
ed upon slave labor and Aristotle
doubted very much whether slaves
had a soul or not.
What a blessed old world it would
be if employes could be induced to
take what they could get from benig
nant employers; rest content forever
in their station in life and realize
that their masters held a stewardship
from God!
What a contrast with this inferno
today when economic discontent is
rampant; when strikes dislocate in
dustry and the workers refuse to ap
preciate the fine philanthropy of
employers! The malcontents insist
upon entertaining the belief that
they have rights; that they have bod
ies which should be pampered and
that they have souls just like employ
ers! Is there no way whereby indus
trial peace can be brought perma
nently to earth and these disturbing
beliefs in the hoi polloi dissipated?
—Nome Worker.
ARIZONA STRIKE SETTLED.
WASHINGTON, Oct. 25.—The
president’s labor committee in the
west has telegraphed a formal an
nouncement of the settlement of the
copper miners’ strike in Arizona.
DON’T DELAY WAR
PREPARATIONS
During the time of war, the con
sideration of the labor question must
be made from an entirely different
standpoint from that taken in times
of peace when there is no danger from
the alien enemy. When the members
of a union strike for higher wages
they should at the same time consider
the fact that they are causing every
enemy of their country to smile with
satisfaction. They are doing the very
thing that the kaiser is now spending
hundreds of millions of dollars to have
them do. To delay the building of a
ship is the assassination of a thousand
troops at the front who are working
for less wages than the man who
strikes is receiving. That isn’t fair. It
is not treating the laboring man at the
front fairly.
Just at this time the house is on fire
and it ill becomes the brother out of
danger to be bawling about the price
he has to pay for a bucket to carry
water to quench the fire while there is
a brother inside being consumed by
the flames! These are times when we
can each afford to cry aloud “My king
dom for a horse.” Every man should,
during these times, exclaim, “Take all
I have for a ship, or an airplane.”
But, whines some pessimist, “These
men are making too much money out
of this ship building business! They
are grafting!” That may all be true
but while the house is on fire, don’t
stop to try a man who is attempting
to steal something from the smoke
house. We will hang him to the near
est telegraph pole after the fire is
quenched. There is only one man who
is a bigger criminal than the wax
grafter and that is the man who as
sists the kaiser in delaying our prep
arations for war.
uu cvci tyj
consider the opinion that the million
soldiers soon in France will have of
you after they return, when you are
pointed out to them as one of the men
who delayed a ship for a single day
that was to bring them food while
fighting the enemy ? How can you ex- j
pect the officers of the law’ to keep
them from taking you by the throat
and choking the life out of you? Will
these soldiers not remember the pangs
of hunger they suffered because the
ship was one day late?
There is no question at all about the
man who is grafting in war supplies
being despised by every one after the
war is over, but it will be an easy
thing to take his ill-gotten gains
away from him. That is one good
thing the war measures that have been
adopted assure us. That’s easy, but
if the kaiser wins, who is going to
make him cough up ? Are the strik
ers figuring, that inasmuch as they
helped the kaiser when the clouds
hung low, that he will make them rul
ers over this part of his world empire
when he wins? There would be some
sense in that view’ if the kaiser were
a just man, but, a fool knows that
should he, with the assistance of those
who handicap Uncle Sam, win the
wrar, he will enslave the world.
Then why assist him ? Why do
the very things that he prays you will
do ? Be it remembered ,nowr, that the
man who attempts to make profit out
of the dire distress the country is in
at this time, either by quitting work
or conspiring to obtain exorbitant
prices for goods, will, after the w’ar, be
loathed and despised by those soldiers
in France who are offering their lives
in defense of democracy for the people
of the w’orld. Whatever other crime
we may have committed while here on
earth we do not want one of those
soldier boys, after returning from
France, to point their fingers at us
and say, there is a skunk that delayed
the ship and caused a hundred boys
to starve to death on the battle field
of France.”
HAMPERING THE WORK.
It is hard for a loyal American cit
izen to understand disloyalty. It is
inconceivable how an American can
hamper the work of the government
at a time when the very life of the
nation is at stake. It appears that
such words as “home” and “country”
make no appeal to some of these tools
of the> kaiser, and it has become ne
cessary for the authorities to admin
ister a lesson in patriotism. The
country at large will hope that the
roundup will be complete.—Seattle
Post-Intelligencer.
FREE SPEECH.
Some time, and it will be a much
cooler epoch when the emotions and
sentiments are more controlled by hu
man intelligence, someone will rise
to explain and suggest the necessity
of tracing the genesis of things as
quite essential to a proper under
standing of them, and equally essen
tial to a proper support of them in
society.
Take this matter of free speech.
Technically how did it originate that
men could not speak the thoughts that
were in them ? That in itself is an in
teresting question, but not more inter
esting than important to the proper
appreciation of the value of a condi
tion in society when men can express
their opinions such as they are with
out fear of molestation or punishment.
Of course we are a long way* off
such a consummation, and right today
there are a good many persons who j
would get mighty short shift if they i
were to say what they think about
profiteers during the war.
BANK OF ANCHORAGE FINDS ITS I
STORAGE PLANTS ARE SMALL
FOR BUSINESS OFFERED.
The railroad switch has been com- :
pleted connecting the large warehouse,
recntly built by the Bank of Anchor
age, with the main railroad line and
today shipments for different mer
chants in town are being received.
The warehouse has a capacity of
1500 tons and all space has been re
served and contracted for. The Bank
of Anchorage has recently purchased
for a warm storage warehouse lot 12,
black 43, from F. D. McCullough.
The purpose of the Bank of Anchor
age in securing warehouses was to
loan on warehouse receipts and rent
space to customers. The only mis
take appears to be that the space in
both warehouses is entirely too small
for the amount of business offered.
ALAMEDA AND ADMIRAL
WATSON ARRIVE HERE
With twenty-one cabin and four
steerage passengers and a large cargo
of freight, the steamship Alameda, of
the Alaska Steamship company, ar
rived Saturday morning from Seattle
and way ports. She made her de
parture for Puget sound at midnight
Saturday. Among her passengers
were: W. F. Rohbach, J. I. Simpson,1
Mr. and Mrs. C. E. Brown, Mr. and
Mrs. L. D. Henderson, R. R. Heuter,
J. G. Watts, J. R. Beeson, J. J. Stur
gis, J. L. Waller, Carl Lind, A. Brand
strom, Fred Larson, F. A. Ross, R. W. j
Hart, S. S. Stannard, Mrs. S. J. Na-!
tion, Tex Jones, L. P. Bergman and
Harry R. Brown.
The steamship Admiral Watson, of:
the Pacific Steamship company, arriv
ed here Saturday afternoon from
Seattle and departed on her return |
voyage Sunday afternoon.
_
WANT MORE WAGES.
CLEVELAND, O., Oct. 25.—The
executive committee of the Order of
the Railway Conductors and the
Brotherhood of Railroad Trainmen,
representing all roads in the United
States and Canada, will meet in Chi
cago probably November 1, to consider
the advisability of requesting an in
crease in wages, made necessary be
cause of the high cost of living.
MURDER ON KUSKOKWIM.
Dan Shea, who recently arrived in
Iditarod from the Kuskokwim, has re
ported to the U. S. marshal at Fair
banks that Louis Huber recently shot
and killed Matt Schranthan, his min
ing partner, on New York creek.
There was no witness to the shoot
ing. At the preliminary hearing be
fore Commissioner Stubb, Huber was
held to answer and released on $5,000
bonds.

WITH THE HIGH SCHOOLS
McCauley Finds Move Toughest of All
By CARL SELL
SUr Staff Writer
Coach Jim McCauley of the
new Einstein High School In
Montgomery County is having
a tougher time selling his house
in Annapolis than he did teach
ing football to a group of raw
youngsters who reported for
Einstein’s first team last fall.
McCauley, who coached Na
vy’s 150-pounders in 1961, took
the job at Einstein and then
set about moving to the Wash
ington area. That was easier
said than done, since he hasn’t
been able to sell his house in
Annapolis.
So Jim commuted the 70-
mile roundtrip each day to Ein
stein. He's still at it, although
prospects of a move to the area
are bright.
Einstein wound up with a
9-0 record in its first season.
McCauley wouldn’t have given
a plugged nickel for the chance
of that kind of record last
August, even though the Titans
played a junior varsity sched
ule.
“I didn’t dream it would be
as successful as it was,’’ Jim
said yesterday. The Titans
wound up their season by beat
ing Whitman, Montgomery’s
other new school, 7-0, in its
final game.
One of the brightest young
prospects in Maryland schol
astic football is the Titans
speedy halfback. Dick Richards. I
A 155-pounder, Richards scored
13 touchdowns last season.
Another top boy at Einstein
is Center Russell Bradshaw,
who was named to The Star’s
All-Suburban Maryland second
team. Bradshaw, a 6-1, 190-;
pounder, was an excellent line
backer, according to McCauley.
McCauley also singled out!
Guard Mike Goodwin, Fullback
Bo Bruno and Quarterback
John Gossage for special praisW ,
■» « » »
Clarke County High School
has piled up 18 straight vic-h
tories in winning the Virginia j
District 10 Group n title forh
two straight years. The Eagles (
won the title this season by |
whipping Warren County, 39-7, j
to finish with a 5-0 record in i
league play. (
Richard Dean, former all-star '
center at Eastern High School,
recently was honored as the I
outstanding lineman at De- 1
Pauw University in Indiana :
DePauw is coached by Tommy
Mont, the former Maryland
coach.
Dean’s biggest achievement!
was kicking the extra point!
that gave DePauw a 7-6 victory
over arch-rival Ball State. A I
junior, Richard is a candidate |
for All-Indiana Collegiate Con
ference honors as a center. i
The award was made to Dean
by the Greencastle Quarterback
Club. DePauw has had two
straight 5-4 records, first win
ning seasons by the school in
20 years. 1
•• * *
One of the hottest football 1
prospects in Maryland is Phil
Petrey, a 6-foot-2, 200-pound <
quarterback from South Ha-i
gerstown High. More than 40 J
schools have indicated an in
terest in the youngster, who 1
complied a phenomenal three
year record.
Petry completed 82 of 140
passes for 1,281 yards and 14 '
touchdowns and ran the ball
137 times for 819 yards and 15
touchdowns while leading
South Hagerstown to a 9-0 rec- '
ord this fall.
Over a period of three years,
I’hil completed 215 of 379 passes
for 2,870 yards and 31 touch-!
downs. He also carried 328
times for 1,789 yards and 20
touchdowns.
The Hagerstown star also is
a top-flight pitcher, and several
major league teams, including
Terp Wrestlers
Trounce Alumni
Maryland's wrestling team
which won the Atlantic Coas'
Conference championship the
last nine years, gave notice last
night that it’ll be tough toi
handle again.
The varsity whipped an
alumni team, 27-0, in an exhibi
tion at Cole Field House and
in the process a former Olym
pic wrestler and two former
ACC champions were defeated
They were Ernie Fisher, Pat
Varre and Gene Kerin, all of
whom may not have been in
the best of shape. On the win
ning side for the varsity were!
Roger Neutze. Ron Maunder.
Sam Bossert, Dave Ott, Nelson
Aaron. Bob Kopnisky, Mands
Soto, Marshall Daub .man ant'
Gary Wikander. •

FIRST GLIMPSE SATURDAY NIGHT
Maryland Cage Hopes
Not Exactly Bright
By STEVE GUBACK
Star Staff Writer
The University of Maryland’s
basketball team tips off its
season Saturday night at home
against Penn State and the fact
that Coach Bud Millikan talks
about spirit, effort and deter- '
mination is a tip off of sorts,
too.
The Terps lost their last eight
games last season and finished
8-17, the worst record in Milli
kan's 12 years at College Park.
The prospects for immediate
improvement do not look bright.
Millikan, who'll pin his hopes
on seven players, says there will
be a shortage of depth and
speed.
“Our kids are going to have
to give every dog-gone ounce
of their effort every second of
every game," he says, “in order ,
to make up for those two big
deficiencies in a league such as
we compete in.”
I This is not necessarily the
old crying-towel approach.
There seems to be a genuine
shortage of firepower while the
rebounding, a serious flaw last
year, is still suspect. The ball
handling also is questionable.
Five Varsity Holdovers
Millikan will build his club
around five varsity returnees
who averaged only a combined
36.2 points a game last year,
plus two untested sophs, 6-
foot-4 Phil Carlson and 6-1
Sam McWilliams.
If the Terps provide any
scoring surprises, they'll have
to come from holdovers Jerry
Greenspan, who averaged 15.2,
and Bob Eicher, a playmaker
who averaged 11.8. Greenspan,
who is 6-6, hit 20 points or
more a half-dozen games last
season, but was marked by his
inconsistencies. In four other
games, he scored six or fewer
points.
Eicher. 6-2, will have to pro
vide the steadying influence.

The O’Connell tournament al
ready has lined up most of the
available teams on an annual
basis.
** * *
Northern Virginia Group 1-A
athletic directors have set up
a five-man committee to study
ways to split the 18-school
league into divisions for all ma
jor sports. Herb Yost, athletic
director at the new Marshall
High, heads the committee.
Serving with him are Rasty
Doran of Alexandria, Jim Coyle
of Annandale, Al Haringer of
Washington-Lee and Mike Mil
lan of Lee.
♦» * *
John Austin. DeMatha’s All-
Metropolitan for two straight
years, has an inside shot at a
future chance to play with the
Boston Celtics. Or at least John
He Is the best defensive player
on the club, an accurate jump
> shooter and perhaps the best
i ball-handler. If either Green
-1 span or Eicher gets in foul !
trouble, the Terps will need
more than a stiff upper lip to I
ball them out.
The other holdovers are Joe,
Barton (4.6 points average),'
Scott Ferguson (2.2) and Con
nie Carpenter (2.4). Ferguson,
originally ticketed for the!
starting center job, is bothered
by a twisted ankle and that re
duces the number of so-called
reliables to six.
Dropouts Hurt Team
The reason Maryland, the
ACC champion only five years
ago, is so shy of talent now is
not easy to uncover. Millikan■
points out only that three con
secutive freshmen teams pro
duced very little.
Academic dropouts, plus the
transfer of a player like Tom
Baxley, who averaged 31 with'
the Terp frosh two years ago,
; are now being felt.
Three players who could help
this club Tom Barrett and
George Suder, the frosh’s top!
two scorers, and Bill Stasiu
latis. who averaged 10.4 with
1 the varsity—are currently side
lined academically. They may
or may not be available Febru
ary 1, when the grades come
out again.
Millikan plans to start Eicher
and Carpenter in the back
court, Barton at center and
Greenspan and the soph, Carl
son. up front against Penn
State. Carlson was the No. 3
scorer on the 8-7 frosh team
and will be pressed into start
’ ing duty, ready or not.
Millikan, in all seriousness,
i says the basketball program at
• Maryland is on the way back.
That must be in refarence to
the frosh. In a scrimmage the
other day, they whipped the
. varsity.
. has opened the door. He’s an
• usher at the Boston Garden
I for the Celtics’ home games.
Austin, who attends Boston
College, and J'-hn Kafa’aus.
, who also played last year at
> DeM< s id now is a student
r at Northeastern, got the jobs
I as ushers through Red Auer
. bach, the Celtics’ coach.

TERPS DRAW
TOURNEY SPOT
Maryland’s basketball
team, which won't partici
pate in a holiday tourna
ment this season, will help
start a new one next year.
The Terps will participate
in a two-day event, De
cember 20-21, 1963, at Vir
ginia Tech’s new 10,000-
seat coliseum in Blacksburg,
Va.
Maryland and host Vir
ginia Tech will be joined
by two Southeastern Con
ference teams. Tennessee
and Louisiana State.
Green Wants
Recruiter Ist
NASHVILLE. Nov. 29 (AP>.—
When Jack Green enters the
academic atmosphere of the
Vanderbilt University campus
as head football coach his first
staff appointment will be a
full-time recruiter.
"If a recruiter does a good
job the year-round, he is the
most valuable public relations
man you can have,” Green said
last night in a telephone inter
view from Gainesville, Fla.
Green, 38, was selected as
the man to succeed Art Guepe
as skipper of the Commodores.
Guepe’s resignation as head
football coach at Vandy be
comes effective after the sea
son-ending game with Tennes
see Saturday.
