WCRLD-WIDE TENSION
Cadets Have Edge
In Field-Goal Odds
Navy beat Army, 13-7, last
year on the field-goal kicking
of Grey Mather, but if the
issue is settled by field goals
Saturday, the odds are in favor
of Army.
Dick Heydt, who’ll wear No.
36 in Philadelphia Stadium,
has attempted five field goals
for the Cadets and made four
iof them. Navy has tried field
goals twice. Fred Marlin, a
sophomore linebacker on the
defensive team, missed one, and
Dave S.iuggerud, a two-way
end, missed the other.
•• • •
All the tension won’t be con
fined to Philadelphia. The
game will be broadcast by
Armed Forces Radio all over
the world and it is likely that
more than one pajama party
has been planned.
Comdr. Vince Anania, officer
representative of the Navy
squad, remembers when he was
stationed in Korea. The party
began at midnight, but the
game didn’t start until 4 a m.
Ships all over the fleet will
carry the game on radio, and
for those few hours everything
will come to a standstill.
And Comdr. Anania can’t
remember a single Navy en
listed man rooting for Army.
** * •
As much secrecy as surrounds
' a Paris fashion house now
cloaks the uniforms Navy will
wear Saturday. Ray Novell!,
i the Navy equipment manager,
promised that something un
usual will be on both the
I jerseys and helmets. The
Middies boldly printed “Beat
Army” on their shoulders last
year and all eligible pass-re
iceivers wore helmets painted a
ifluorescent orange.
Army's famed Chinese Ban
dits. the defensive unit in
Coach Paul Dietzel’s three-
; platoon system, have the in
- signia of a coolie with a hatchet
! on their shoulder, while the
offensive unit, or Go team,
’ wears a missile shoulder patch
’ And the Corps of Cadets has
been known to wear red coolie
hats at the games.
Novell! has been wearing a
' coolie hat himself this week,
’ just to rile the Middies.
•» • *
I Even though he claims End
t John Ellerson was elected Army
> captain off his performance
[ against Navy last year, Wayne
r Hardin, the Navy coach, claims
he’s glad Ellerson recovered
from his injuries in time to
play Saturday.
’ "I know what getting into
’ this game means to the kids,”
. Hardin said.
t** * *
r It will be unusual if someone
doesn’t get demerits this week
r at Annapolis. Upperclassmen
r have been rousing Plebes at all
5 hours of the night and sending
r them on such errands as com
; mandeering a truck and push
’ ing it in front of Bancroft Hall,
1 the principal dormitory.
I Signs hang from buildings
, enticing the Middies to “ Think,
Hate, Kill Army.’” The locker
t room also is plastered with
. similar slogans.
«* * •
Tackle Jim Freeman came up
. with a sore back in a practice
j session and spent yesterday in
I the Academy infirmary, but
he’s not too concerned about
’ the prospect of missing Sat
.' urday’s game.
.! ••It’ll take more than this to
> i make me miss it,” he said,
t Freeman is on the defensive
l unit.
*« * »
i If the Middies parade past
St. John's College, its neigh-
■ bor in Annapolis, Saturday,
i they’ll be dispatched to Phila-
■ delphia in the usual manner.
Liston Leaning
To Baltimore
BALTIMORE, Nov. 29 (AP).
Heavyweight Champion
Sonny Liston has begun nego
tiations to fight the return title
bout with Floyd Patterson In
Baltimore’s new 14,000 - seat
Civic Center.
Jack Nilon, Liston’s adviser,
talked with Promoter Lou
Grasmlck Monday about pos
sible dates for the bout.
Grasmick said if Patterson
doesn’t agree on Baltimore.
Liston is ready to fight either
Cassius Clay or Ingemar Jo
hansson here.
Under the agreement signed
before the first title fight, Pat
terson has the right to name
the location for the rematch.
However, Liston’s camp feels
■the contract has been broken
since no money has been placed
I in escrow.
It is customary for the stu
dents there to print ’harassing”
signs in Greek and flaunt them
before the Midshipmen.
But as one 20-year-old soph
omore with uncombed hair,
wearing a pair of open leather
sandals and no socks in 30-
degree weather and a bushy
beard, said: "They get back
at us. Every time the band
goes past it plays the St. John's
song and nobody here knows
it.” He also said that for ob
vious reasons they play the
Gillette razor blade song.
—SOMMERS.

JUNEAU, ALASKA. WEDNESDAY, MAY 28. 1930.
MEMBER OF ASSOCIATED PRESS
PRICE TEN CENTS
REPUBLICAN NOMINEE FOR GOVERNOR DISBARRED
SENATOR WALSH
CHANGES HEART
ON DRY ISSUES
Will Abide by Referendum
Vote in Montana If
Question Submitted
WASHINGTON, D, C.. May 28.—
United States Senator Thomas J. ,
Walsh, Democrat of Montana, an
ardent dry, today said he is willing
to submit to the people of his home
state a referendum on Prohibition.
Senator Walsh said he “will stand
by the result.”
The announcement caused as
much comment as the one made
yesterday by Senator Wesley Jones,
another ardent dry, when he said
he was for modification of the
Prohibition Amendment if modifica
tion was voted by the people of his
state, Washington.
LIQUORMRTY
CAUSES BLAZE;
INVESTIGATION
Coliseum in Oklahoma City
Destroyed — Fire
Persons Dead
OKLAHOMA CITY. Okla., May
28.—State fire investigators today j
delved into reports that a liquor .
party was responsible for the fire'
yesterday that destroyed the Coli
seum where A1 Smith made his
Presidential campaign speech.
The loss is $300,000. Five per-!
tons are dead as a result of the
fire.
It is rumored that a drinking |
party was on in the north end of j
the building shortly before the fire
started.
15 TORTURED,
THEN KILLED
Coolie Women Are Victims
of Mob During Strike
in Rangoon
LONDON, May 28.—The Rangoon
correspondent of the Daily Mail
reported 15 Indian coolie women
were massacred by the fighting
coolie mobs during rioting which
has already taken a heavy toll of
lives as a result of the dock strike.
The women, the correspondent said,
had been tortured before killed.
RANGOON. May 28.—Two per
>:ns were killed when the Police
opened fire today on a party of
Indian Moslems during a new flare
up as a result of yesterday’s dis
orders.
The Moslems help up a tram car
and assaulted Burman passengers.
20,000 in Parade
Seeking Right
To Gamble
t- --—
SAN SEBASTIAN. Spain, May
28.—A parade of 20,000 at this Roy
al resort, seeking reopening of the
gambling facilities at the casinos,
turned into a political demonstra
tion against the Berrenguer Gov*
ernment.
The Police charged and fired into
the crowd.
Thirty persons were injured, four
perhaps fatally, and twenty arrests
were made. The others of the
huge crowd then dispersed.

EUROPEAN UNION PACT
WOULD TAX AMERICANS
By JOHN EVANS
(A. P. Staff Writer)
PARIS, May 23 —A federation of
European slates, supported by head
| taxes upon visiting Americana and
; ether non-Europeans, has been put
i into ooncrete form and submitted
: to ail European governments by
l Count R. N. Coudenhove-Kaiergi,
| president of the Pan-European Un
ion of Vienna.
It Is offered as basis for discus
sing the European union idea re
| cently evolved by Aristide Briand.
I foreign minister of Prance. M.
Briand is proceeding with his plan
by collecting suggestions from the
various governments under author
ity of the League of Nations, the
count’s scheme being entirely un
official.
Abolition of war through disarm
ament and the elimination of the!
political and economic friction are
given as the underlying principles i
of the proposed pact.
In general the idea is to estab- |
lish a governing council with a'
chancellor, treasurer and various1'
secretaries as active executives.1
There would be a legislative assem
bly with membership alloted ac
cording to comparative populations;
of participating stales, and a su
preme court of three chambers of:
five judges each, selected by the as- I
sembly and irremovable.
This court would enforce fed-1
eration laws and decide disputes be- j
tween member states. Disputes with ,
nations outside of the new league i
would be referred to the League of
Nations or the Permanent Court of
International Justice at The Hague.
While the federation would be
pledged to disarmament and its
members would guarantee to re
spect the covenant of the League
of Nations and the Kellogg pact
for renunciation of war, the count's
draft would also unite the military
resources of Europe. Members
would regard an attack upon one
of them as an attack upon their
own territory and would “undertake
to protect the attacked federal
member by political, economic and
military means.”
The proposed “Federated States
• Continued on Page Eight)
TAILOR’S HEAD GEAR
SA VES TURKISH CROP
KOZAN, Turkey, May 28,—A rain
that fell on fourteen semi-fezzes
saved the spring crops of this An
atolian district, according to the
local tailor who fashioned the for
bidden headgear of old Turkey, and
thereby landed in jail.
For months drought menaced the
farms and the prayers of fezzless
Moslems brought no rain. Then the
tailor of Kozan, one Djemal Effen
di, concocted a cross between a fez
and a turban, the two types of
headgear which the Kemalist gov
ernment has outlawed.
When he had made fourteen , of
these, the tailor clapped one on his
own head and distributed the oth
ers among thirteen villagers. Each
man astride a camel, the proces
sion solemnly rode to the fields
and prayed for rain. Two hours
later it poured.
The tailor, realizing he had struck
a gold mine, announced the an
nual grasshopper plague could be i
staved off by fez-turbans which
could be procured for two measuresj
of wheat ea.’h.
Then the republic’s gendarmerie
got wind of the business arid hauled
him to a cell.
irchduke Otto May
Wed Italian Princess
w""1 ■' ~i
Political circles in Vienna discuss
:he possibility of the marriage of
Archduke Otto and a Princess of
;he Italian royal family. Maria,
,-oungest daughter of Victor
Emanuel, who is sixteen, was men
tioned. Otto’s claim to the throne
are believed to have fallen
through, and it 's reported he will
Boon make a trip to South Amer
ica, where it is expected he will
Stay for some time. I

ARCHDUKE OTTO
WANTS THRONE
Reported Lad Will Issue
Declaration Assert
ing His Rights
BUDAPEST, May 28.— Reports In
Registist circles are that Archduke
Otto, eldest' son of the late ex
emperor Karl of Austria, on his
next birthday, November 20, will is
sue a declaration asserting h i s
rights to the throne of Hungary.
He will be 18 years of age on his
next birthday. He is now being
educated in Belgium.
Makes Salt in N. Y.
For Gandhi; Gesture
Of Love for Leader
NEW YORK, May 28.—Amid the
cheers and Jeers from a noonday
crowd, Raymond Duncan, brother
of the late Isadora Duncan, dancer,
dipped a bucket of sea-water at the
Battery to make salt for Gandhi.
He will take the salt to Gandhi
when he sails for Paris next week.
Duncan said the act was a ges
ture of love to the Indian leader
from American sympathizers.
TARIFF BILL
IS RETURNED
TO CONFEREES
Another Revision of Flex
ible Clause Must
Be Made Out
WASHINGTON, May 28. — The
tarilf bill went back to a confer
ence today for another revision of
the ‘flexible clause.
The conferees will meet tomor
row.
The bill was returned when Vice
President Curtis and President of
the Senate sustained a point of
order raised by the Democratic op
ponents contending the conferees
exceeded authority by drafting a
compromise on the flexible pro
vision. The language contained
neither the House nor Senate draft
of the bill.
WRANGELLGETS !
BUILDING SITE
ON CONDITIONS
■— I
Must Provide School for:
Indians in Order to Ac
cept Site Offered
By an Act of Congress approved
by President Hoover on May 9, the
i town of Wrangell is given a school
1 building site conditioned upon the
community providing educational
facilities for all Indian children in
the town, it was made known here
today. The land conveyed by the
Act is the site of the present Bu
reau of Education school at Wran
gell, and that building was also of
fered to the town.
The tract embraces an area of;
42,785.5 feet known as the United
i States School Reserve Number
Three. The town, in order to avail
Itself of the offer must agree to
provide all Indian children of
Wrangell and vicinity with edu
cational facilities in the same de
gree that is enjoyed by all other
i children.
It is understood that the Wran
gell Indian school will be discon
tinued in case the town accepts
| the site and building.
COUNTERFEIT
MONEY FOUND
NEW YORK, May 28 —Expertly
counterfeited $10, $20 and $50 gold
certificates of spurious value to
the amount of more than $1,000,
000 were seized with a large litho
graph press by Secret Service oper
atives on the upper floor of a
I Brooklyn apartment house.
FORMER JUNEAU MAN
REPORTED ARRESTED
BY SEATTLE POLICE
Albert Mathews, said to be a
I former Juneau resident, was arrest
!ed last week in Seattle, in company
i! with William Gordon, and they are
i being held in Tacoma as robber
| suspects, according to word received
j here. The arrests were made at
: the request ,of Tacoma authorities
who believe the two are responsi
ble for a series of robberies there.
Eaoh of the men carried a re
volver, while a search of Mathews
! brought to light, police said, three
. ,watches, three valuable rings, and
. a number of unset diamonds. Oor
: don had two watches in his pos
. session. They refused to reveal
; where they got the valuables.
Mathews is said to have resided
i here a number of years and to have
; relatives living here now.
A. Van Mavern is making the
round trip to Sitka on the Admiral
Rogers. He will continue south
to Ketchikan on the steamer.

> • The tallest structure in the •
. • world, the Chrysler building. •
l • has been opened formally. It •
. • is 1,036 feet high, 46 feet •

con, Archbishop of Rheims, 87 j

Dog Causes
Master to
Lose Life
' _
• BOISE, Idaho, May 28.— • j
• Fred Bailey, aged 44 years, • ,
• arose to let his dog in the •
• house. Bailey bumped his •
• head against a dresser, fell • (
• to the floor, dying within a • |

GRAF ZEPPELIN
STARTS NORTH
Leaves Pernambuco This
Morning for Havana
and Lakehurst
PERNAMBUCO, Brazil, May 28 —
The Graf Zeppelin left for Havana
at 9:13 a.m. today. The huge air
craft circled over the city and then
started north.
Departure of the Graf was de
layed several hours as rain made
her too heavy to lift for the flight
on the leg of which is 4,500 miles.
Commander Eckener said he
hoped to reach Lakehurst Sunday.
j Ford Gives His
\Cure for Business
I Depression
! DETROIT, Mich., May 28.—The
' shortest cut to relief of business
, depression, in the opinion of Henry
I Ford, is intensive development of
| agriculture and manufacture look
: ing to quantity of production from
'soil. He said: "It will take just
! as long to get rid of industrial de
1 pression as it took to accumulate
it."
Says Methodist Hoard
Supported Action for
Change, Enforcement
i _
WASHINGTON, D. C.. May 28 —
j Believing Attorney General Mit
ichell to be "by far the driest the
l Department of Justice ever had,”
i Deets Pickett told the Senate Lob
t by Committee today the Methodist
I Board of Temperance supported
j legislation to take Prohibition En
forcement out of the Treasury De
partment.
.
GEORGE JOSEPH
LOSES RIGHT TO
PRACTICE LAW
State Supreme Court of
Oregon Causes Unpar
alleled Situation
LOOPHOLE IS OFFERED
HOWEVER TO NOMINEE
Another Portland Attorney
Loses His Rights—
Peculiar Case
SALEM, Oregon, May 28.
—A situation unparalleled in
the history of Oregon has de
|vclcped by the Supreme Court
disbarring George W. Joseph,
Republican nominee for Gov
ernor, from practicing law in
this State.
i The Supreme Court also
disbarred Thomas Mannix,
another attorney of Portland.
Both are disbarred perm
anently.
I Joseph was given an oppor
tunity for reinstatement on
condition he retract accusa
tions filed by him against two
members of the Supreme
Court.
| No such loophole was of
fered Mannix.
j Joseph and Mannix brought
disbarment proceedings
against each other. Joseph
started the proceedings ac
cusing Mannix of deceiving
! the Supreme Court in the
l Wemme Endowment Fund
! case at the time when Jus
1 tice Rand granted Mannix an
j option to purchase a mine in
! Baker County for $300,000.
j Joseph sought the nomina
! tion as “vindication” of the
| disbarment recommended by
I Portland attorneys.
__
CART. TURNER
SETS RECORD
Reaches Glendale from
New York in Fast Time
—Makes One Stop
| GLENDALE, Cal., May 28.—Capt.
iRoscoe Turner landed here at 7:46
o'clock last night establishing a
new record, East to West, in a
one stop flight. His total elapsed
time from New York was 18 hours
43 minutes and 34 seconds, beat
ing by half an hour, Frank Hawk's
nonstop time. The stop was made
at Wichita, Kansas.
Rheumatism of King
Showing Improvement
LONDON, May 28.—An oficial
statement today from Buckingham
: Palace said the King's rheumatism
j shows definite improvement.
Laughs Himself
Into Jail; He
Is Still Laughing
KODIAK. Alaska, May 28.—Jacob
j Kabelnikoff. native of Kanatak,
aged 21 years, literally laughed him
self into Jail and is still laughing.
He will be sent to the hospital at
| Mornlngside as mentally unsound.
His weird laughter alarmed the
I citizens kt Kanatak. so on their
j way to the court at Valdez, his
(brothers brought him and left him
i here.
The authorities became perplexed
' and placed him in Jail on a vag
' rancy charge.
: He continued to laugh.
Kabelnikoff carried a butcher
i knife in his belt. The guards got
ft.
It is believed that' with proper
; treatment he will recover. An of
' ficer is coming here from Valdez
' for him to take him south to the
lasylum.

WARREN WILLIAM looks well in black and white check coat with
jray flannel trousers. His shirt is white, his tie and handkerchief
a deep blue.
Paris Cable Reports, Fashions of
Special Interest to
Americans
By WILMA LAVILLE
Not all Paris fashion news Is
important to us. So many times
the Parislennes wear exotic, ex
treme things that we would
never wear. Furthermore not
all French women are well
dressed... any more than all
Americans are well dressed.
But fashions do originate in
Paris, New York or Hollywood,
with Paris still holding a bit of
an edge over the other two
places. So when we get a good
fashion report with wearable
clothes mentioned, the kind we
import, wear and copy, it is
of considerable significance to
us.
Such a report is on my desk
today, and it has so many
things mentioned that are of
special interest that we can go
into nearly every item, and
get ideas from it.
Colored Gloves
Colored crocheted gloves are
seen with black linen suits.
That’s a smart summer thought,
and also suggest that colored
crocheted gloves would be good
with white linen suits. Bright
strawberry pink is a favorite
over there with black linen.
My own idea is to get yellow
gloves and hat for a white
linen.
Split skirts are beginning to
be worn for afternoon. I be
lieve that’s of more significance
than an idle glance may sug
gest. Split skirts are very pop
ular for evening, and are seen
for afternoon a bit in America.
In another season or so, my
prediction is that, they will be
worn here a good deal. These
skirts, which were designed by
Marcel Rochas, are straight
and narrow, split a few inches
at the hem on either side.
Earrings Popular
Earrings in ball, clip or pen
dant types are popular for
Colored Slips
By all means wear silk slips
to match your sheer dresses
whenever you can. The down
town stores are showing silk
crepe slips, plain tailored or
lace trimmed, in all the popular
pastels, and at very modest
prices. The plain slips look best
under most dresses. They’re
good for eyelet batistes, and all
other “see-through” dresses.
Plain slips look best under
most sheer dresses in summer,
no matter what the color, so
be sure you have at least one or
two in your wardrobe. It is
also better to wear a well-cut
bias slip under a bias cut dress,
and a straight slip under
straight cresses. it doesn't
take as many slips for your
wardrobe as that may infer,
for one good bias slip and one
good straight slip in white will
do the trick.
Don’t miss the three fall pages of fashions for men and women in the Saturday .Times. They are in the feature section, “Hollywood on Parade.
afternoon. One afternoon
every woman at the Ritz bar
wore them. Big diamond hoops
are worn in the evening. So
are diamond stars or cres
cents for the hair, and they
even appear in the afternoon,
showing under the side of a
black hat. A similar orna
ment may be wom on the hat
itself.
Satin is wom a great deal
for evening. It has predom
inated at many gala affairs,
with satin gowns even outnum
bering sheers of various types.
The gowns wom were new, not
winter satins, which makes it
doubly significant.
A note in advance of fall is
the continued popularity of
dyed furs. They were wom
here in the spring to some ex
tent, in Paris much more.
Watch for them this fall. Furs
dyed blue are extremely popu
lar, and other colors are com
ing in.
For fashion information, call
Miss Laville at District 7000, or
write, enclosing self-addressed,
stamped envelope.

V o>3w ,:i |mM
YOU CAN crochet this
lovely luncheon set your
self, from free instruc
tions. Just send a self
addressed, stamped envel
ope to Wilma Laville at
The Times for instructions.
Cotton crochet thread
is used and the whole
set will not cost much.

White Pique
for Men
White pique suits for men
are making an appearance
around town. They should be
popular as soon as men learn
the merits of pique, for it has
many.
Pique, made of cotton, is
snowier white than linen, and
does not crush as badly unless
you’ve invested in very high
priced linen. That means that
you can wear a pique suit
much longer than you can
wear linen.
There’s enough body to pique
to make a well-fitted suit, and
it is durable and sturdy. Me
dium or small wale pique is
used for these suits. They’re
inexpensive, only about sl3,
and will give you excellent
service.
Ruffles From Spain
Ruffles, and we’re using mil
lions of them, are Spanish more
than anything else. A skirt
ruffled to the knees or further
up is pure Castilian. Some of
our loveliest evening gowns are
of white organdie, or dotted
sheer material of some kind,
with ruffles, and perhaps a
huge flower posed at the neck
line or at the waist.

ESTHER RALSTON is too lovely in an exotic cape of uncurled ostrich feathers. It is
in dark brown and white, and she wears it over a rose beige crepe gown. This is an ex
treme fashion, worn only for very formal affairs.
Fathers Dine Out in Navy Coats
And White Trousers
By BEAU McALLISTER
Dick Davidson presents a
“gigolo” type of smoothness
these days. His blond hair —
very light from the sun—and
skin very tan—he looked well
in a white linen suit with a
double-breasted coat. With this
he wore a blue shirt and dark
striped tie. Dick’s coat, I no
ticed, was cut in the highly
approved lengthy fashion.
* ♦ ♦
Emmons Smith has gone in
for yellow very strenuously.
Saw him wearing a white linen
suit, blue shirt, yellow tie and
yellow handkerchief to match.
This was during business hours.
When he steps out in the eve
ning, he favors a dark blue
coat and white flannel trousers.
♦ * *
“Tommy” Thomas, Helen
Marye’s husband, prefers a
white linen coat with dark
trousers.
•♦ ♦ *
There is a sort of whip-cord
serge in white which is find
ing much favor among those
who don’t go in for white flan
nels—or white linen. This ma
terial is very good looking and
.trousers made of it worn with
an odd coat—are very nice.
But after looking in the dining
and drinking rooms of various
hotels —and noticing masculine
dress at most of the clubs, it
isn’t difficult to come to the
conclusion that white linen is
the “peoples’ choice.” You see
it everywhere—“men in white.”
Long Island and New England
may cling to tweeds—but not
Washington.
Everything from “mess”
jackets to ties in white linen.
This mess jacket fad has cer
tainly got the gents by the
throat —and I can’t imagine
why. They look awful on nine
out of ten men who wear them.
A fashion stolen from military
dress—it still looks like a uni
form on the nlajority of men—
only it hasn’t as stiff an ap
pearance, which gives it the
“bellhop” touch. Bad news—
very bad news!
* * ♦
Bob Riggs, Bank Fleming and
Britton Browne dine out in
dark blue coats and white
trousers. Young Britton imi
tates his father.
* * ♦
Gray S. Foster Is a good
“clothes horse.” He looks well
in one of those new dark blue
linen suits—cut very well. With
this he wears a shirt in fine
dark stripes—and a white col
lar.
* ★ ♦
Why Is it that so many of
these young Washington boys
are as sloppy in dressing as
they are in dancing. Their
collars always need to be
pinned—or their hair brushed
—or their suits pressed.
* * *
George Galbraith likes white
line n—and Francesca Mc-
Kenney.
♦ ♦ ♦
The Austrian Minister, Ed
gar L. Prochnik still prefers
white flannels to shorts for
tennis. Never mind, by an
other year he will probably be
converted.
Tablecloths
Following the fad for using
drapery materials for dresses,
and dress materials for draper
ies or slip covers, you may use
a plaid linene table cloth to
make a coat, dress, or blouse.
Some separate jackets and
blouses of plaid tablecloth linen
are being sold in town, and are
very chic. Evening gowns of
the same material, fringed and
simple, are seen here and at
smart resorts.
For Leftovers
Sometimes in spite of all that
can be done vegetables will be
left over. When this happens
use them to make a vegetable
salad. Combine carrots and
peas, either buttered or in a
rich cream sauce. Leftover
vegetables and leftover meat
combine to make a scalloped
dish pr a few vegetables heated
in meat stock make delicious
soup.

The Vogue for
Sailor Hats
PARIS.—The vogue of sailor
hats is increasing daily. How
ever, in spite of its ever-grow
ing popularity, it has not be
come too monotonous because
of the infinite variety of shapes.
The new sailor hats range from
cartwheels and rickshayr hats
to tiny rakish things that
should be worn over one eye
after the fashion of the late
fiO’s.
Most of these hats are ac
companied by a ribbon dis
posed at the back and encir
cling the nape of the neck, some
times in knotted effect, some
times with an extra bunch of
flowers. This ornament is not
only extremely becoming, espe
cially when It is disposed over
short curls, but it is also ex
tremely practical to hold a hat
in place that is on one side
of the head, where it would
never remain without the as
sistance of this strap because
of its very shallow, if not prac
tically inexistent crown.
Rayon taffeta with embroid
ered dots or covered with
stitches, sometimes in plaid
designs, is very fashionable for
this type of hat. However, it
is now replaced by exotic
straws, particularly by rayon
straws and very bright paillas
son straws. Grosgrain ribbons,
also lacquered rayon ribbons,
constitute the favorite trim
ming of these novel hats. Some
models to accompany tailleurs
often show quills of lacquered
fabrics instead of ribbon orna
ments. Flowers are used for
trimming the more elaborate
sailor hats and are often dis
posed as a small bouquet in
front or a garland underneath
at the back. Wild flowers still
hold the preference.
Seersuckers
In case you haven’t gotten
on to it, seersuckers this year
are disguised so you’d never
know them. If you object to
the unpressed look of the old
striped affairs of the past few
seasons, or if you’re just plain
tired of them, then you should
look again.
There are tiny checked and
plaid designs that look for all
the world like tropical worsteds.
They have the good habit of
all seersuckers of not wrinkling,
but they do not look cottony.
They come in dark colors that
are serviceable, yet. they’re no
more expensive as to laun
dering.
USEFUL FROCK
A useful frock is of very sheer
black chiffon with vertical tuck
ing all the way down. It has a
huge collar and jabot of white
mousseline de soie.

JOAN CRAWFORD wears a dress of heavy corded silk with flannel
coat, both in white. They are designed by Adrian, and have rope
trimming in chalk blue.
What You See
In Paris
PARIS—The Oriental influ
ence is noticeable in several col
lections, especially on coats to
accompany both day and eve
ning dresses. The three-quarter
swagger coat inspired by Chinese
styles, and known as the
“coolie” coat, is getting more
and more popular and is spon
sored by all the leading design
ers for summer costumes, each
of them adding a note of per
sonality. These new swagger
coats are usually in contrasting
effects with the dress. They
are seen in flax linen, rayon
pique, cotton pique, shantung,
and even in heavy dull rayon
crepe on the more elaborate
models.
Lyolene disposes a white rayon
pique coolie coat with a little
straight collar, fastened in front
with two twin navy blue com
position buttons, over a navy
blue cocktail dress in taffeta.
Bruyere shows the same sort of
swagger coat in natural or vivid
colors of flax linen over interest
ing dresses in soft woolen. She
fastens these coats on one side
with several buttons of pyro
xylin plastic composition, start
ing on the straight collar and
continuing over the shoulder
yoke.
Helen Hubert accompanies
some of her very elaborate after
no o n or informal evening
dresses in heavy dull rayon
crepe with coolie coats either In
the same material or in contras
ing taffeta, with a quilted band
in front forming trimming and
a large composition motif to
fasten the little straight collar.
BLACK FLAT CREPE
A charming black flat crepe
evening frock has a print of
white, gray and vivid red flow
ers. It features the deep, square
neckline and has brief kimono
sleeves.

Summer Belts
For Men
There’s a growing feeling
among men that belts shouldn’t
cut them in two. They rather
resent that black line, some
what like a snake coiled about
the waist, which a black belt
gives with a white suit. So out
goes the black belt for such oc
casions !
Now, with a white suit there
are smart white leather belts.
They may be plain or with an
other leather set in—or with
black oi' brown trimming in
neat design. There are also
belts covered with Palm Beach
cloth, to go with linen or Palm
Beach suits. They’re scarcely
perceptible.
Woven of Cord
We’ve already pictured the
sports belt woven of strong cord,
some of them of the fishing line
type of cord, which are cool,
comfortable, and have enough
give to make them very easy to
wear. They come not only in
white, but in the smart colors
of the season, such as yellow,
royal blue, and so on. They
make a very up-to-date note on
a man’s sports costume.
Striped belts are good looking,
too, and if they are cool and
summery looking, they do not
in the least give that heavy look
that a leather belt gives, even If
they are in a dark color or
colors.
Many men like belts of leather
with elastic put inside in some
miraculous way, “live leather”
is the name, I believe, which are
firm enough, but give with the
body, so that they’re comfort
able even after a big meal.
THREE-TIER CAPE
Cool winds do blow even In
the summer time, and so one
woman, who is addicted to navy
blue and white, finds much use
for a three-tier cape of navy
blue dyed fox.

EDITORIAL PAGE
OR THE
Cardinal Hayes Celebrates
Fortieth Anniversary of Priesthood
i <- i i
\
1 y§B3
HIS EMINENCE, CARDINAL HAYES, WHO CELEBRATES
THE FORTIETH ANNIVERSARY OF HIS PRIESTHOOD
Millions of good men and women rejoice with Cardinal
Bayes in the anniversary which marks for him the end of
forty years of useful work as a priest. It is not necessary
to praise Cardinal Hayes, or to tell the people what he is.
His life for forty years has been before them, he has
(worked among the people, and with them. He is an earnest
man, a good priest, a good American, one whom his fellow
citizens of all faiths and nationalities delight to honor.
The elevation of Cardinal Hayes to the highest position in
the Catholic Church in this country proves the wise
of the Catholic Church.
Promotion in the church is for merit, as the case of
THE TENEMENT HOUSE AT 15 CITY HALL PL., IN NEW
YORK CITY, WHERE THE DISTINGUISHED HEAD OF
THE CATHOLIC CHURCH IN NEW YORK WAS BORN
Cardinal Hayes proves, and the selection, whether of bishops,
cardinals or popes, has been a democratic selection.
This explains largely, to the lay mind, the power of_the
Catholic Church, persisting through so many centuries.
When the choice of kings and emperors depended on the
accident of birth, and the further accident of legitimacy,
the Popes, heads of the Catholic Church, were chosen for
merit and ability, by the College of Cardinals, as keen and
intelligent, highly educated and discerning a group of men
as could be found.
The people of New York, and millions outside, wish for
Cardinal Hayes many more years' of life, usefulness and
happiness.
Enforcing the Gun Law
Police and Dealers Co-operate
The apparent determination with which police are setting
about the enforcement of the new firearms law is encour
aging.
Already one dealer has been arrested under the provisions
of the law and one person alleged to have given false in
formation in seeking to purchase a weapon has been appre
hended.
Rigid enforcement of this law cannot stop all crimes com
mitted with guns, but it certainly will have a deterrent effect.
Merchants of the city who deal in pistols have shown a
commendable spirit of co-operation in the enforcement of the
law. In fact, one of the two arrests was made possible by
this co-operation.
The law places some slight burden on dealers, requiring
that they keep complete records of gun sales and that they
ifail to complete sales until the police have investigated.
1 That burden must be borne if the city is to be protected
i properly from those who would use guns in the commission
of crime.
Fortunately the law does not interfere to any great extent
with the purchase of weapons for defense by law-abiding and
respectable citizens.
If the criminal can be prevented from obtaining a gun, the
femes! man soon will not need, ona.

LISTEN, WORLD!
By Elsie Robinson
Characters Aren’t Natural— ♦
They’re Acquired
He’s been telling me what a
poor worm he is
How he’s just naturally weak
and-wobbly
And how he envies people
K
KLSIE ROBINSON
who are bom.
with strong
characters.
The whole
thing sounds
familiar. I’ve
heard that
alibi thou
sands of
times. Even
used it my
self, until I
didn’t have
the heart to
kid myself
any longer.
For, of course,
that’s all it
is—just kidding—feeble flim
flam for floppy folks. Not a
word of truth in it.
NOBODY IS BORN WITH A
STRONG CHARACTER.
Characters aren’t bom;
they’re made.
We all start weak and wob
bly. We start with fear in our
hearts and ignorance in our
brains; and because of that
fear and ignorance, we’re all
little savages at the start, little,
stupid, greedy, dishonest, cow
ardly savages. And then, start
ing at the scratch, we must
begin to acquire our civilized
characters.
Bit By Bit
That process is always diffi
cult. It’s never easy for any
one. No one is naturally good,
or brave, or honest. Each one
of us must learn to be these
things, bit by bit.
Literally, bit by bit.
There’s no such thing as ac
quiring character in blocks.
You can’t learn how to be
brave, honest, kind or sensible
in a week, or a year, or a
score of years. You can’t
achieve these things by one
act, or hundreds of acts. You’ve
got to pile act upon act—little
acts that seem insignificant and
unessential.
BUT NO ACT OF YOUR
LIFE IS INSIGNIFICANT OR
UNESSENTIAL.
EVERY ACT OF YOUR LIFE,
EVERY DECISION YOU
ONCE OVERS
N.
Thinking or Dreaming ♦
Isn’t it true that when you
say you are thinking, you are
only allowing your imagina
tion to form impossible dreams.
The real problems are thrust
back in your mind and pleasant
min'd pictures present possible
easy methods of obtaining what
you desire.
How childish this is! 1
A sheer waste of time—
nothing to be gained by it.
In fact much Valuable time
is lost when you should be
thinking out something prac
tical.
The more you encourage
these day dreams the less you
get down to reality and your
real duty. ,
MAKE, HAS A MEANING AND
HAS AN EFFECT ON YOUR
CHARACTER.
EVERY ACT, EVERY DE
CISION HELPS TO BUILD UP
YOUR CHARACTER, OR
BREAK IT DOWN.
Your character is the result
of ALL your acts; of ALL your
decisions.
But most of us don’t realize
that. We think the little things
we do don’t matter. We aren’t
interested in doing the little
things. We don’t care much
how we do them. It’s the Big
Ambition that matters.
Each one of us has some Big
Ambition —some cherished
dream of achievement. And it’s
always a fine dream, without a
flaw in it. There never yet was
a man who wanted to become a
villain, nor a woman who want
ed to become a fool. We all
want to be great examples some
day, and we’re all sure that we
will be what we desire.
Will Come True
Yes, some day it ill all come
true. And in the meantime?
Well, in the meantime we
muddle along, doing tiresome
things, and, because they seem
so unimportant, doing them
sloppily. Making little decisions,
and, because they seem so triv
ial, making them carelessly.
And so sloppiness and care
lessness grow on us, as habits,
and presently our morals are
as fumbling as our hands—and
our thoughts are as fuzzy as
the domes that cover them.
But all the time we’re dream
ing of the thing we want to be,
envying the people who are al
ready like that, and thinking
that, because our dream is
splendid, it must surely come
true.
BUT IT DOESN’T!
WE NEVER BECOME WHAT
WE WANT TO BE.
WE ONLY BECOME WHAT
WE CHOOSE TO BE.
AND WITH EVERY CARE
LESS ACT, WITH EVERY
FOOLISH DEC ISION, WE
MAKE THAT CHOICE.
Don’t wish you were strong.
Don’t envy strong people.
BE STRONG!
START AS THEY STARTED!
START NOW!
(Distributed by Kinr Features,
Syndicate. Inc.)
How much intensive thihk
ing have you done in your life
time?
Even when your thoughts are
sensible they are surface
thoughts.
You have never concentrated
to a point where you could
completely forget the outside
world.
No, you keep that roving eye
and that roving mind that
stamps you as shallow.
Teach yourself to think by
forgetting what others are do
ing and doing, something con
nectedly and earnestly your
self.

I MARRY-GO-ROUND
By Helen Rowland
Harvest Moon-ings
Men don’t fall head-over-heels
in love any more; they just
HEIL
/v .JSWi
"tJsoME 4
4 E MS
wade into it carelessly and
lightly—and seldom more than
ankle-deep.
Women are funny. Even when
a husband fusses or complains
all through his meals and goes
to sleep right after the dessert,
his wife gets sore if he doesn’t
come home for dinner. There
just must be something mascu
line around the house to give it
a finished look.
Troubles never come singly—
at least they don’t seem like
“troubles” until you’ve been
married to them for a while.
“The course of true love” runs
so “smooth” for the average
man, these days, that he gets
bored and switches off onto
another course, before he gets
half started.
A man seldom goes into mar
riage with his eyes open. He
waits until he has been blinded
by some blow of fate or dazzled
Three-Minute Journeys
By Temple Manning
Even if you have to give up ♦
some other part of your itin
erary, do not fail when in
France to visit the castles along
the Loire—those lovely cha
teaux that have furnished in
spiration for so many beauti
ful homes in America. Until
a few years ago it was possible
to walk along Fifth Avenue in
New York and pick out houses
that were absolute copies of
French castles. The chateaux
of Blois, Azay-Le-Rideau and
Amboise have been especial
favorites with many architects.
But the incentive of build
ing a magnificent home is not
essential to appreciation of
those lovely poems in stone
built centuries ago on the ver
dant banks of the river Loire.
One has only to be a lover of
beauty to enjoy a visit to the
chateau country of France.
While most of its castles were
built between the fifteenth and
seventeenth centuries, all are
of the decorative type of archi
tecture and each is distinctive
and original.
The trip through the chateau
country takes the tourist
through the provinces of
Orleanais, Blesois, Touraine and
Anjou. In Touraine one finds
the best opportunity to visit a
great number of chateaux in
one area. Indeed, the city of
Toun itself is well worth more
♦ by some turn of fortune, and
then just totters or staggers
into the arms of the woman
who happens to be nearest.
A pessimist is not always one
who has to live with an opti
mist; it is sometimes a woman,
who is married to an unquench
able amateur-humorist.
Call a man good and he’ll sus
pect you are laughing at him;
call him dangerous and he’ll
wonder if you are trying to
snare him; but call him “differ
ent” or “unusual,” and he’ll be
lieve any other flattering fic
tion you choose to tell him.
Every man must have been
born with the soul of a juggler,
to judge from his burning pas
sion for trying to keep two
\ yOVKt
$ F-.
love affairs perfectly balanced
at the same time.
Just about now, the bright
summer romance which lit up
all the scenery for a girl, is be
ginning to look as faded and
washed-out as her bathing suit.
(Copyright, 1932. King’s Feature
Syndicate. Inc.)
than a casual visit, and ever so
many motor tours start from
there in various directions.
Tours, situated on the banks
of the Loire, has a population
of over 75,000, and excellent
hotels. Not far up the river
from Tours is the royal castle
of Amboise, where Charles VIII
was bom and died. Built on a
terrace overhanging the Loire,
one can see from afar the enor
mous towers of the castle sil
houetted against the sky. A fea
ture of this castle is its beauti
ful chapel in the Flamboyant
style.
Down river from Tours are
the Fifteenth Century castles of
Luynes and Langeais, the latter
built for Louis XI and still in a
perfect state of preservation,
with an interior furnished in
pure Gothic style. Here in 1491
the marriage of Charles VII
and Anne of Brittany was cele
brated.
There are five beautiful cha
teaux in the valley of the Cher,
each well worth a visit. Sur
rounded by moats full of water,
from the midst of exquisite gar
dens rises the grimly impressive
Chateau of Villandry, a feudal
stronghold.
Nearby is Chenonceaux, one
of the loveliest of all the old
castles, built right over the
river Cher and in rarely beau
tiful surroundings.
What Labor Knows:
Butler’s Indictment
By Claude G. Bowers
Author of "The Party Battles of the Jackson Period,” “The Struggle
for Democracy in America,” “Jefferson and Hamilton,”
“The Tragic Era,” and other important works.
THE Hoover high com
mand, running true to
the form of 1928, is basing
its hope on the cynical phi
losophy of a cheap politician
who said that the way to
win is to “lie and stick to
the lie and retiterate it time
and again and the fool pub
lic will believe it.” Never
at this stage of a campaign
have so many deliberate
falsehoods been set afloat,
and uttered by men hold
ing high and responsible po
sitions in the Government.
We sometimes hear that a
campaign in England is
pleasantly free from slash
ing attacks on political op
ponents. This is not true.
The Lloyd Georges, Winston
Churchills, Stanley Baldwins
and Ramsay MacDonalds at
tack one another on the hus
tings with a ferocity that
American politicians in their
positions seldom do.
But these attacks are never
deliberate falsehoods; for the
English spirit of decency and
fair play would react against
the slanderers; and public
opinion would conclude that
men so utterly irresponsible
and reckless with the truth
would be unfit for office.
It remains to be seen
whether the American peo
ple will be as complacent
with the Hoover high com
mand’s methods as they
were four years ago, when
it followed the lowest and
the meanest methods ever
adopted in the history of
American politics.
But these reckless dema
gogues of the Hoover high
command unquestionably
have “guts”—if no sense of
shame and no capacity to
blush. Four years ago, a
low undercover campaign
was waged against Al
Smith by these people
along lines too mean to
mention. Senator Moses
played his part. The pub
lic still remembers a cer
tain historic letter of the
Senator’s, proving how
deeply he was involved in
those disgraceful transac
tions based on bigotry and
hate. And now this same
Moses has the effrontery to
say that Al Smith, the vic
tim of Hoover strategy of
that sort, is going to help
vindicate the very men, in
cluding Moses, who ma
ligned and defamed him.
That, we say, is “guts.”
Coolie Labor
Impressed by Hoover
And now the committee of
the Hoover high command,
unable to answer Garner’s
masterful letter of accept
ance, is busy disseminating
the most flagrant falsehoods
about him. The latest is
that he is a foe of labor.
Four years ago, Mr. Hoover
boasted at Newark that he
had employed 100,000 men
and never had a strike.
That sounds impressive—un-
til we inquire into the char
acter of the labor he em
ployed.
It was coolie labor in
China.
It was worse than coolie
labor in Burma.
And these men, veritable
slaves, were so pleased with
Mr. Hoover’s attitude to
ward them they did not
strike! Imagine!
Mr. Hoover’s attitude to
ward labor during the many
years of his expatriation
goes far to explain his at
titude toward the unem
ployed today, and explains
the use of bayonets and
tanks in driving from the
nation’s capital the jobless
bonus veterans, and the
burning of their shacks.
The American Federation
of Labor knows this to be
true.
Mr. Hoover’s consistent
attitude for three years has
been one of cold and callous
indifference to the misery of
WASHINGTON, D. C„
SEPTEMBER 10, 1932
♦ the jobless. He has opposed
every plan that would put
them to work on public con
struction with Federal
money. He killed the pros
perity loan. He killed pro
posed compromises in plans
to give them jobs—until he
had whittled one down to
the last possible reduction.
He was opposed even to
that, but he signed the bill.
And it begins to appear
that he signed it with his
tongue in his cheek. Not a
wheel has been turned.
Many weeks have gone and
nothing has been done. And
now Ogden Mills, that
famous friend of the toiler,
announces lightly that about
the first of October he will
know whether he will re
lease the money. The money
for the great corporations
and banks was found and re
leased in the twinkling of
an eye. But it is a matter
for interminable deliberation
when hungry men, denied
their inalienable right to
work are involved.
And the American Feder
ation of Labor knows that.
Butler Arraigns
Hoover Policies.
Nicholas Murray Butler,
in his annual address at
Southampton, has drawn an
indictment of the outstand
ing policies of the Hoover
Administration. He was
speaking as a patriotic
American of keen intelli
gence, and not as a politi
cian. And he denounces the
tariff policy of Mr. Hoover,
■which has sacrificed a lucra
tive foreign trade, and is
directly responsible for the
unemployment of about 25
per cent of the jobless. It
is not a “protective,” but a
prohibitive tariff that we
have had forced upon us by
the lash of Grundy and the
subserviency of Hoover. And
Dr. Butler says:
“The fact that at the
present time there are some
10,000,000 Americans with
out work illustrates pretty
clearly the measure of suc
cess which this system has
attained in establishing and
maintaining an American
standard of living.”
And the American Federa
tion of Lqbor knows that.
Maligned Smith;
Now Seeks Favors
And, unintentionally, per
haps, but no less clearly,
Dr. Butler indicts the Hoover
Administration when he
says:
“It has already been neces
sary to change in large
measure the original attitude
of our people toward the
natural resources of the
Nation.”
Mr. Hoover has not found
it necessary—read his record
on power.
And Butler continues:
“The public forests, the
public lands, the resources
of water supply and of
power can no longer be left
to private exploitation at
the urge of the profit-seek
ing motive for the simple
reason that to do so would
put a group of fortunate in
dividuals in control of some
of the necessities of the
people’s life.”
that is precisely •what the
Hoover administration wants
and fights for.
It is precisely what Mr.
Roosevelt contends against.
And that is the reason
that Street is for Mr.
Hoover, despite its open
jeering at his incapacity for
leadership a few months
ago.
There are places in the
Butler speech that seem
like an echo of the Roose
velt speech at Columbus.
Mr. Roosevelt’s attitude
on present day problems and
needs is that of intelligent
progressive thinkers in all
parties; Mr. Hoover’s is that
of the age of unrestrained
human greed of the few,
which has all but wrecked
. our civilization.
title: The Washington times. [volume]

text:
THE LARAMIE REPUBLICAN
MEMBER OB THE ASSOCIATED
VOL. XXI NO.l 10.
FIVE ?" ,Q
IN SENATE
MEMBERS GET BUSY AND NUM-
BER RUNS UP TO THIRTY-
NINE AT OPENING SES-
SION THIS MORNING.
Senator Holliday Among Number, With
Bill Providing for Appointment of
State Chemist and Assistant State
Chemist —Both Houses Ballot
Separately for Federal Senator at
Noon on Tuesday.
By L. C. Hills.
Special to The Itepuhllcan.
Cheyenne, Wyo., Jan. 23. —Both the
house and senate reconvened today
and had- busy forenoon and afternoon
sessions. Five bills and one resolution
were introduced in the senate. Senate
Files Nos. 8 and 9 were held up one
day. The appropriation bill was set
for 3 o’clock this afternoon.
New Bills in Senate.
The following bills were introduced
in the senate today:
Senate File No. 35, By Mr. Kendrick,
relating to branding stock. I
Senate File No. 36 f by Mr. Kendrick,
providing an eight-hour law for women
and children.
Senate File No. 37, by Mr. Taylor,
employment of stenographers in the
senate.
Senate File No. 38, by Mr. McClel
land, concerning the ditch water com
missioner.
Senate File No. 39. by Mr. Holliday,
providing for the appointment ot a
state chemist and assistant.
Carpenter Mertiorial.
The joint memorial by Mr. Carpen
ter, asking congress to protect pro
hibition states in interstate liquor
shipments was read.
Bills Ordered Printed.
In the house'bills Nos. 25, 28, 29 and
30 were ordered to be printed. In com
mittee of the whole, bills Nos. 28, 17,
26 and 8 were recommended for pas
sage. Much time was spent on the
primary bill.
Senator Tomorrow.
Each house will take a separate bal
lot on United States senator tomorrow,
at noon, and a joint ballot on Wednes
day. The opposition to Senator Clark’s
re-election is weakening and his elec
tion seems inevitable.
Woman Member on Floor.
Mrs. Bellamy of Albany county, the
only woman member of the legisla
ture, made an address on the primary
bill this afternoon.
FATAL FIRE IN
OMAHA HOTEL
SIOUX CITY MAN SUFFOCATED
WHEN THE CONFLAGRATION
THREATENS THE MIL
LARD EARLY TODAY.
Three Hundred Guests. Asleep at Time
and Barely Escape, Five Being Se
riously Hurt—Former Teacher of
Languages in University of Wis
consin Found Dead When the Ex
citement of Morning Ended.
Omaha, Jan. 23.—One man is dead
and flv c others are in a serious con
dll ion from inhaling smoko aa the re
sult of a fire early today in the Millard
hotel. Three hundred guests were
asleep in the hotel when the lire broke
out and many barely escaped with
their lives.
Thomas J. Fields of Sioux City, for
mer instructor In languages at the
University of Wisconsin, was suffocat
ed by smoke.
SOLD SOME LANDS
TO EASTERN MAN. .
John F. Behl today sold to H. W. i
Dezotell, for 2,800, the south half of i
the northwest quarter of section 27, i
township 18, range 74, <
NOVELIST SHOT.
Unknown Man Wounds David Graham
Phillips and Kills Himself.
New York, Jan. 23. —David Graham
Phillips, the novelist, was shot and
probably fatally wc.uudcd this after
noon, while on his waj from his home
to the Princeton club. The shooting
was done I'? an unknown man. who
committed suicide. Phillips was tak
en to Belleview hospital.
JUSTICE PERFORMS
HIS FIRST CEREMONY.
Justice Reid performed his first mar
riage ceremony this afternoon, the
happy couple asking him not to give
the names. The license was issued at
2:35, the couple going immediately to
the justice’s office In the city hall.
RIOTING
IN CHINA
TROUBLE STARTS WHEN REPORT
IS RECEIVED THAT COOLIE
HAD BEEN KILLED BY
THE BRITISH.
American Consul General Mosher
Wires Government to Have Gun
boat Sent to Hankow to Protect
American Interests—Natives Kill
ed in Rioting and They Are Now
Boycotting all the Foreigners.
Hankow, Jan. 23.—American Con
sul General Mosher today wired his
government asking that a gunboat be
sent to this place to protect American
interests in the event of the continu
ation of the rioting that began yes
terday.
Coolie Killed by British.
The disorders followed the report
that the British police had k.’lled a
coolie. Considerable damage was
done and British and German marines
were landed to defend the foreign
community. Probably twenty Chinese
were killed during the rioting and a
number of foreigners were injured.
Chinese Troops on Guard.
Today the foreign concessions are
protected by Chinese troops. The ri
oters have been checked, but have in
stituted a boycott against all foreign
ers.
FIGHT ON PLAGUE.
Suburb of Harbin to Be Isolated as
First Step in Work.
Harbin, Manchuria, Jan. 23. —Fud-
ziadian, a suburb of Harbin, and the
principal plague spot of Manchuria, is
to be isolated as the first systematic
step toward exterminating the plague
that has grown more threatening ev
ery day.
A Russian physician, who has just
completed an inspection of Fudziadi
an, discovered three plague sources in
the neighbornood of which seven
thousand persons reside.
Corpses in Streets.
Corpses were piled high in the
streets and in the yards adjoining the
homes. Last week there were fifteen
hundred deaths in Fudziadian.
TO STUDY DISEASE.
Foreign Countries Asked to Send Ex
perts to Affected Districts.
Pekin, Jan. 23. —The government is
sending a note to the Chinese minis
ters abroad instructing them to invito
the government to which they are ac
credited to send experts to the affect
ed districts in Manchuria to study the
Bubonic plague, and apply means of
extermination. The government of
fers to bear the expenses of the cru
sade.
damage suits filed
FOR A LARGE SUM.
Groesbeck & Eby, attorneys for the
plaintiffs, have filed suit In the district
court for Bertha and Herman Mahs
against the Pioneer canal company, I
for $95,000 damages for failure to sup- 1
ply ,water to their land for the past
eight years. Another suit was filed
against the Union Pacific for S7BO
damages from fires starting from
sparks from locomotives. The Mahs
own land under the Pioneer canal. i
LABAMIE, WYOMING, MONDAY, JANUARY 23, 191).
LEAGUE
FORMED
PROGRESSIVE REPUBLICANS GET
I TOGETHER AND FORMULATE
DECLARATION OF PRINCI
PLES FOR FIGHT.
Senator Bourne of Oregon Chosen as
President and Declaration Signed
by Nine Senators, Thirteen Mem
bers of House, Governors of Six
Republican States and Others—
Norris Vice President.
Washington, Jan. 23. —The organi
zation of tne Republican Progressive
league by tne piogressive repuuiican
senators, representatives, governors
and others, an organization that will
seek to “tight for the estaolishment of
popular government,’’ was announced
aere today.
Declaration Signed.
The plans of the new league were
concluded Saturday night, when the
declaration of principles were signed
and these officers were elected:
The Officers.
President — Senator Jonathan
Bourne of Oregon.
First Vice President —Representa-
tive George Norris of Nebraska.
Second Vice President —Governor
Charles Osborn of Michigan.
Treasurer —Charles R. Crane of Chi
cago.
Nine Senators Sign.
The declaration of principles is
signed by nine republican United
States senators, the governors of six
republican states, thirteen members of
the house and others, it says:
Claims of Organization.
“Popular government in America
has been thwarthed and progressive
legislation strangled by the special in
terests, whicn control the caucus, the
delegates, the conventio* and the
party organizations, and 'ough the
control of the machinery of govern
ment dictate nominations and plat
forms, elect administrations, legisla
tors, representatives in congress and ;
United States senators and control the
cabinet otneers.
Legislation Baffled.
“Under the existing conditions, leg
islation in the punlic interest has been
baffled and defeated. Just in propor
tion as popular government has in cer
tain states superseded the delegate
convention system, and the people
nave assumed control of the machin
ery of government, has the govern
ment Become responsive to the popu
lar will and pi ogressive legislation nas
been assured.”
Some of Its Plans.
The league advocates the election of
United States senators by-direct vote,
direct primal lessor the nomination o.
all elective officers; the direct elec
tion of delegates to the national con
ventions with the opportunity for a
vote to express choice for president
and vice president; amendment to
state constitutions providing for the
initiative, referendum and recall, am.
a thorough and general corrupt prac
tice act.
Will Assist States.
The declaration says the league will
co-operate with the progressives in the
several state, and whereve accept
able will render assistance in promot
ing the organization of state leagues
aid in the preparation of appropriate
bills and resolutions ajid furnish
speakers and literature.
Carey One of Them.
The signers include Governor Carej
of Wyoming, Merl D. Vincent of Colo
rado, William Allen White of Kansas
Governor Stubbs of Kansas, Governo
Johnson of California, Governor Mr
Govern of Wisconsin, Governor Aid
rich of ivebraska, Senators Lafollette
Beveridge, Bristow, Cummins, Brown
Dixon of Montana and Gronna.
PROHIBITIONISTS.
Leaders Gather at Chicago for Nation
al Committee Meeting.
Chicago, 111., Jan. 23. —Scores of na
tional prohibition party leaders from
nearly every state In the union are in
town to attend the meeting of the na
tional committee today. Plans for the j
party’s presidential ca paign of 1912
will be considered. |
WOMAN IS
SHOTDEAD
ITALIAN MOTHER JUST HERE
FROM MILAN IS KILLED
THROUGH A WINDOW
IN NEW YORK.
Came to Live With Sons and Police
Believe Black Hand Bullet Was
Intended for One of Them—One
Death Each Year in Block in
Which These Two Italians Reside
—The Charltd|| Case Again.
New York, Jan. 23. —Another Black
“ Hand murder was added to the long
i list in New York just after midnignt.
> Mrs. Francisca De Chilla,. an Italian
l mother of sixty years, who came to
■ New York from Naples only a week
I ago to live with two sons, both pros
perous merchants, was shot and killed
as she sat by an open rear window at
» ner sons’ home in an east side tene
-1 ment. The police believe the bullet
was intended for one of the sons, both
of whom have received threatening
tetters. According to the traditions of
the east side Italians, the Black Hand
exacts one Jife every year from the
• olock on Christie street where the De
Chilla;; live.
CHARLTON CASE.
Further Hearing in Matter of Habeas
Corpus Proceedings Begun Today.
Trenton, N. J., Jan. 23.—Further
hearings in the matter of the habeas
corpus proceedings to prevent the ex
tradition of Porter Charlton to Italy,
werp begun in the United States cir
cuit court here today before Judge
Rollstao. Charlton is wanted on the
charge of wife murder, having thrown
the body of Mrs. Charlton, who was
Mary Scott Castle Charlton, into Lake
Como after the murder.
COUNTY HCES
A REAL CRISIS
THE GRAND JURY UNDERTAKES
PROBE OF ALLEGED BUYING
AND SELLING OF VOTES
AT DANVILLE.
Investigation Depending Upon Two
Men, Judge of Circuit Court and
Foreman of Grand Jury—Disclos
ures That May Pale the Adams
County Sensation—Books for Con
victs and Money to Buy Them.
Danville, 111., Jan. 23. —Vermillion
county’s grand jury today faced a sic
nation whicn may piove a crisis in
.ne political affairs of the county. The
jutcome of this week’s investigation
nay be said to depend upon two men
Circuit Judge Kimbrough and Fore
zian Isaac Woodyard of the grand
jury.
Judge jn Charge.
Judge Kimbrough will have charge
of the court in which the vote sellers
appear if the matter approaches the
nature of the situation developed in
Adams county, Ohio.
Foreman Makes Statement.
Fear of the sellers of votes of sum
mary punishment if they were found
out is oelieved to be the factor which
will “start things.” Foreman Wood
yard has said repeatedly that he will
go into the question with all the
means at his disposal.
BOOKS FOR CONVICTS.
League of Library Commissioners
Meeting Response to Appeals.
New York, Jan. 23.—It’s an ill wind,
etc., and its zephyrs are floating good
American coin right into the cash reg
isters of several big wholesale book
sellers of this city, as a result of the
recent decision of the League of Li- (
brarv Commissioners of several states j
to see that convicts in federal pris
ons have a choice of books of their
own selection for the prison libraries.
Boom for Books. I
The book publishers declare that as .
a result of the decision there has been
a decided boom in the demand for
“The Life of Jesse James,” “Achieve
ments of the Younger Brothers,” |
“Alias Jimmy Valentine,” “MemoriesJ|
of a Yeggman,” “Safes I Have Crack- .
ed,” etc. Poetic desire finds expres-|
slon in a demand for Lovelace’s “To
Althea, in Prison.”
LOSESHUNDRED
IN BIG BATTLE
GENERAL DORANTES AND TWO
HUNDRED MEN ENTER NAR
ROW DEFILE AND FIRED
UPON FROM HILL.
I —
' Revolutionist Flying Squadron Fur
nishes Retreating Force and
Draws Mexican Regulars Into Am
buscade—Retreat Ordered but
Half Force Mowed Down by Piti
less Fire Before They Get Back
i Presido, Tex., Jan. 23. —(Sunday, via
. Marfa). —That fully one hundred gov
, ernment. troops were killed in a light
two days ago at Ojinaga was the re
port received here today. It was stat
ed that the heavy casualty list was
due to an ambuscade.
Right Into Trap.
A flying squadron of the revolution
■ ists is said to have decoyed -a large
i force under General Dorantes into a
trap. The insurrectos entered a nar
, row mountain gorge and the federal
troops followed.
Met Deadly Fire.
When at least two hundred of the
pursuing troops had entered the gorge
a deadly fire was poured into then?
from the heights.
Retreat Ordered.
Retreat was ordered, but before
General Dorantes’ men could escape
at let fit half their number had been
killed and others wounded.
Three Days’ Fight.
The fighting between Ojinaga and
CuchLlio Parrado lasted three days,
starting when a small band of insur
ectors were routed at Coyama January
16th. Os the two hundred federate
who went into the mountain pass only
forty escaped.
Back to the Hills.
The rebel loss was only five killed,
according to their reports, Dorantes
has retired to the foot hills, where he
is awaiting reinforcements.
Prisoners Cared For.
A number of wounded rebels and
federate have been taken to San Juan,
rhe insurrectos cared for the wound
ed federate, treating them with the
same consideration as their own men. •
TENNESSEfSENATOR
IS ELECTED TODAY ■
INDEPENDENT DEMOCRAT RE
GARDED AS CANDIDATE OF
FUSRONISTS IS CHOSEN.
I
Luke Lea, Lawyer and Publisher, Suc
ceeds J. B. Frazier, Breaking the
Deadlock and Defeating Regular
Democratic Party —Sheehan Lack
ed Thirteen in New York Today.
Nashville, Tenn., Jan. 23.—The
deadlock in the Tennessee legislature
over the election of a United States
senator to succeed J. B. Frazier was
broken today by the election of Luke
Lea, an independent democrat, regard- ?
ed as the candidate of the fusionists.
Mr. Lea is a lawyer and publisher. •
i
SHEEHAN LOSES.
Lacks Thirteen Votes of Majority in
New York Legislature.
Albany, N. Y., Jan. 23.—The fifth ’
jo’nt ballot for United States senator <
today was without result. Sheehan i
was snort thirteen votes of a majority. <
EIGHT PAGES
HELPING
. SHIPPING
' —
IMPROVED FACILITIES TO LATIN
AMERICAN REPUBLICS BE
ING URGED IN MEETING
AT WASHNGTON.
Congress Called By National Associa
tion of Manufacturers, Which is
Conducting Campaign for Exten
sion of American Manufactures
Abroad—Would Like to Replace
American Flag on the High Seas.
Washington, Jan. 23. —Improved
shipping facilities and communication
with the Latin-American republics and
other countries calculated to enlarge
the foreign trade of the United States,
are sought by the delegates to the Na
tional Merchant Marine congress,
which met here today.
Called by Manufacturers.
The congress was called by the Na
tional Association of Manufacturers,
which has been conducting a cam
paign of education on the extension
of American manufactures abroad.
The Opening Address.
President John W. Kirby of the lat
ter organization delivered the opening
address, in which he urged the pass
age of the ship subsidy bill as a neces
sary method of extending our foreign
trade.
Hobson and Humphrey Heard.
Representatives Hobson of Alabama
and Humphrey ot Washington also
urged legislation looking to the restor
ation of the American flag on the high
seas.
ATTACK ON ROOSEVELT.
His Ideas on Conservation in Appeal
to the Supreme Court.
Washington, Jan. 23. —“My Policies”
as regards conservation will be the
subject of a vigorous attack in an ap
peal to be made to the supreme court
of the United States today, which will
declare unconstiutional the creation
of the big forest reservations of the
west.
The Light Case.
The fight has arisen out of the at
tempt of the United States to enjoin
bred Light, a Colorado cattleman,
from “allowing his cattle to trespass”
on Holy Cross forest reserve in that
state.
Injunction Issued.
Despite Light’s contention that'the
formation of the reserve without the
consent of the state was in violation
of the federal constitution, and that
action for trespass could not be main
tained under the Colorado laws unless
the land in question had been fenced,
the circuit court of the United States
for the district of Colorado issued the
injunction.
Goes to Supreme Court.
The case, which began under the
auspices of the Roosevelt administra
tion, has been appealed to the supreme
court.
MANY ARE KILLED IN
COLLIDING OF TRAINS
PASSENGER AND COAL TRAINS
COME TOGETHER NEAR HOP
KINSTOWN TODAY.
Three Coaches Loaded With Miners
Going Up to London to Attend a
Convention, Soldiers Recovering
Bodies of Eight Men and Three
Children from Debris of Wreck.
Cardiff, Wales, Jan. 23. —A passen
ger train collided with a coal train at
Hopkinstown, near Pontpriod, today.
Three coaches were telescoped and
many passengers were killed and in
jured.
Eleven Bodies Recovered.
Soldiers recovered the bodies of
eight men and three children from the
wreckage. Those on the train includ*
ed a number of miners on their way
to London to a conference of mine
owners.
title: The Laramie Republican.

text:
2
PLAN TO SCRAP
NAVIES DOESN’T
ML TO 11, 5,
Suggestion of French Naval
Expert Turned Down
by Wilson.
AMERICA IS WARY
Small Nations Anxious to
to Get Naval Powers to
i Weaken Fleets.
t.
BY DAVID LAWRENCE.
* Copyright. by The Sen Antonio Light.
, Washington. D. C., April 7.—The
propose! of the president of the navel
commission of the French senate that
all tl*e big navies of the world be
scrapped and in its place be created
an international police force to which
all countries shall contribute so as to
make an armed league of nations, will
hardly get the approval of the United
States government.
The same suggestion, in principle,
was made at the Paris peace confer
' enee by the French delegation and re
. jected by President Wilson. Since that
time President Harding has in his ad
dresses to Congress unequivocally dis
approved of any alliance of nations
based on armed force and has insisted
that the peace of the world can better
be established through the moral force
„of an enlightened world opinion.
With both Democratic and Rrpubli
• can spokesmen of such prominence
against the idea, the suggestion of an
• other international naval conference
• r.
with that proposal as a basis does
not meet with favor here. When the
Washington conference was in ses
sion to limit naval armament it was
> considered bnpracticable to discuss
‘ the scrapping of all navies, because
; the principal nations which had navies
to serap would not agree to any such
discussion as all felt committed to
the theory of a defensive fleet.
The time was not ripe for an aban
donment of all naval armament with
‘ no police force >n its stead for the pro
. trotion of each country.
A Pacifist Plea.
* On its face the statement of Gns
’ tav DeKerguczcc is in line with the
’ clamorings of pacifist opinion which
, believes in abolishing all weapons as
a certain insurance against war. but
the distinguished Frenchman is not n
member of that school of thought and
,is believed to be interested only in
.the scrapping of th» big navies, name
1 !y the«e if the United States and
•Great Britain, principally the latter's.
because of the state of Franco-British
.relations, and suggests in its place a
• new ratio and an international police
• force The crux of his argument is
‘contained in the following paragraph:
“If the Allies will follow a pro
posal for a general disarmament of
all the warld's fleets and replace them
with an international one. we shall
be the first to agree to let the United
States furnish to this international
fleet ten ships. England ten. France
six and Italr nix.
England then will have peace and
not until then, let u« create while
there is time a real League of Na
tions. The present one is useless he
cauee it is unarmed. Arm a new one
and it can command peace.”
When Woodrow Wilson first
broached the idea of a League of Na
tions at the Paris conference, the
French insisted that if it would be op
erated with an international army and

By the Associated Press.
Jjondoii. April 7.—Five sturdy
mothers, each pushing a perambulator
freighted with protesting baby, cross

The contest is the outgrowth of a

end speediest baby carriage chauf
feurs. The contestants were cheered
by a crowd around the parliament
buildings as they got away at dawn

They expect to reach Brighton by
sunset. .

her offspring, who had been loudly

“SPIRIT WAR DEAD"
PICTURES SHOWN TO
NEW YORK AUDIENCE
Audience Gasps as Arthur
Conan Doyle Exhibits
Weird Photographs.
New York. April 7.—A weird pho
tograph, described by Sir Arthur
Conan Doyle ns that of spirit war
dead, myriads of grim faces hovering
above the cenotaph of the Unknown
Sodier in London during the tw>
minutes of silence for prayer, drew
gasps of astonishment from a large
audience in Carnegie Hall last night
when he gave a lecture on spirit
ism.
Some women sobbed and even scof
fers were thrilled. • •
“Do you see the dead creeping
through’/” Sir Arthur asked, point
ing to the spectral faces on the
screen.
"You can see them everywhere.”
There were two photographs, de
scribed as taken by Mrs. Dean, an
English medium.
The first, a snapshot, showed the
great crowd standing bareheaded be
fore the cenotaph. A (faint luminous
patch appeared over the throng.
The second picture showed count
less heads of sad-visaged soldiers
floating above the memorial. The
spectators were blotted Out. Some
faces were blurred, but others were
distinct. The general impression of
the audience was that the faces were
those of men who had been killed in
battle.
“I don't wonder that you are
moved,” Sir Arthur said, when the
sobbing and buzz of hysterical whis
pered comment interrupted his re
marks.
"I consider it the greatest spirit
picture in the world.”
He declared the plates l*d been de
veloped immediately and that there
was no chance for fraud.

FIND WHITE POWDER
Police Unable to Solve
Mystery at San Luis
Residence.
Mrs. Ignacio Guajardo, 25. 2202
San Luis street. Prospect Hill, and
her 16-niontbs'-old baby were found
unconscious, apparently under influ
ence of some drug, when Justo Gua
jardo. husband, reached home at 12:20
Saturday morning.
Detectives Phil Fox and Ad Kohr,
first to reach the residence after an
alarm had been sent to central bead
quarters. found the house bad been
robbed. A window had been “jim
mied" and several bundles of silk gar
ments and silverware, valued at $6OO
bad been piled in the rear yard.
Detectives are mystified over the
discovery of a strange powder which
they found sprinkled over the bed
in which Mrs. Guajardo lay in a
heavy stupor. The powder, too, had
been sprinkled over the bedding of the
cradle in which the infant had been
put to sleep. The powder resembles
sugar or salt. Detectives will have it
analyzed.
Mrs. Guapardo was revived after
artificial respiratory methods. The
child, seemingly, was in an extraor
dinarily deep sleep. Both mother and
child were pronounced out of danger
several hours later.
While robbery evidently was the mo
tive, officers admit the presence of
what appeared sugar or salt, or, per
haps of both, was a baffling feature.
Tests made at detective headquhrtars
of samples failed to produce the slight
est evidence the powder is dope.
"It is one of the strangest cases
we have been confronted with in some
time,” said Detective Fox. “Until the
nature of the powder is determined
a solution of its bearirg on the case
is impossible.”
Justo Guarjardo, according to his
report to police got response when he
knocked on th* door. He went to the
rear of the house. la the darkness
be stumbled over a heap of bundles.
These proved tn be clothing and silver
ware, taken from the house and
wrapped in bedding and sacks. The
back door was o|>en.
Unable to Give Details.
Entering the house he turned on
the Hk* ts and tried to arouse his
wife and baby. A glance showed
the drawers of bureau* dressers,
etc., had been searched nnd r. portion
of the contents scattered about the
floor. He communicated with police
headquarters and called neighbors.
Mrs. Guarjardo was just regaining
consciousness when officers reacheu
the l.ome. Treatment administered by
her husband and neighbors restored
her senses nnd the baby, too, respond
ed to the efforts. Both, apparently,
seemed drowsy from the effects of
some drug.
The wife was unable to account
for her predicament, snyiug she had
retired early with her baby and had
slept soundly. Guarjardo told of-
Sccrs he saw no one as he approached
the house. Detectives believe his re
turn home frustrated the intruder or
intruders from hauling away the
bundles of valuables.
Would Evacuate Northern Saghallen.
By K. R. BOGER.
Special Cable tn the San Antonio Light
and the Chicago Dally News
Copyright, 1823.
Tokio, April Opinion favoring
the evacuation of northern Saghalien
is growing among Japanese military
authorities who point out that the
continued occupation of part of the
island tends to create misunderstand
ing among foreign countries regarding
Japanese policy. Official quarters,
however, are opposed to evacuation
proposals on the ground that it is
inadvisable to change the government
policy which already has been an
nounced to the world. The question
is soon to be taken up for formal
discussion by the cabinet council.

GOLDEN ROD AS A REMEDY
Plant Dedicated to St. Augustine of
Hippo Was Once Used by
Physicians.
St. Augustine of Hippo, the great
doctor of the church whose festival
fell on August 28, is a sadly neg
lected saint among modern ritual
ists, and even occasionally confused
with the namesake who insulted our
British bishops. Yet he is pro
nounced by Hooker "without any
equal," and regarded by Harnack
as "the first modern man." He has
been better honored in the floral
world, for to him is dedicated the
golden rod or wound wort (Solida
go virgaurea.) Old Gerarde, with
whim this was a favorite remedy,
tells us that golden rod fetched a
high price as a foreign herb until
discovered growing near London,
when the plant was neglected. He
adds the caustic comment: "This
verifieth our English proverb 'Far
fetcht and dear bought is best for
ladies,' or for fantastical physi
cians." Before tobacco the golden
rod also furnished snuff for our me
dieval forefathers - Westminster
GazettI
STRENUOUS EMPRESS.
The empress dowager of China is
a woman of spirit. One of her chief
recreations is wrestling with the
women of the court. In her palace
is a vast apartment set aside for the
practice, and each afternoon she
has a turn or two with her attend
ants. Some time ago it was sug
gested to the empress that fencing
would be a variation. She agreed
to the proposal, and a European
drill sergeant was engaged. But
the first lesson settled the em
press' views. Fencing was too tame.
After the demonstration she went
up to the instructor, took his foil
from him, flung it to the other end
of the room, and seized the sergeant.
After a severe struggle she made
him touch the ground with both
shoulders.
18S HEAD OF HER FAMILY.
The Dutch woman of South Afri
ca is the head of her family and
must be obeyed by husband as well
as children. She marries very young
and has a large family. The wom
an of the cities is very different,
having a good education, and when
she finishes in Europe she is a most
attractive creature, with great ca
pacity for enjoyment. She is likely
to be musical, but not literary, as the
Dutch of that country have not
reached that phase of, development.
The women of South Africa are
very much taller than the women of
Holland, and the city-bred girl is
generally slender and rather pretty,
which the Boer woman never is, be
ing fat and figureless at 30.

always bragging and cackling about
what they are going to do before
hand."

CAN TRUNDLE -HEAVY LOADS
Chinese Coolies Able to Transport
Nearly Half a Ton on Peouilar
Wheelbarrow.
In the wheelbarrow used in China
the wheel is set in the center, and
this supports practically the entire
load, while the handles are support
ed in part by a strap or rope over
the shoulders of the man who oper
ates it.
As a result the coolie in China
will transport nearly half a ton on
his wheel. Wheelbarrows are much
used in the country, where the roads
are but little developed, and we
have heard that passengers some
times make the entire trip from
Shanghai to Pekin, a distance of
600 miles, by barrow.
A two-passenger barrow will make
about 20 miles a day, and the coolie
is content with a pay of about 20
cents a day, or an average of about
half a cent a mile for each passen.
ger. On the level, well kept streets
of the foreign quarters of such cities
as Hong Kong, Shanghai and Pe
kin the wheelbarrow coolie will
struggle along with a load of six or
even eight people.
TWO GREAT ORATORS.
As an orator Demosthenes was
head- and shoulders above the Ro
man. The great Athenian stands in
a class all by himself, if we are
to believe the consensus of learned
opinion. Cicero, it is said, prided
himself on his faculty of extempor
izing at need, but probably trusted
little to it on great occasions; while
with Demosthenes it was the rule
never to speak without the most
careful preparation. The speeches
of both were spoken without manu
script. They would never have
made the reputation they did if they
had been tied down to their notes.-

COLOSSAL CHERRY TREE.
The historic giant cherry tree of
this county is located on the Aber
nethy place, southeast of Newberg.
A measurement was made the other
day which gave it a circumference of
eight feet 11 inches four feet from
the ground-this against the Hub
bard tree, with its eight feet two
inches two feet from the ground.
From limb to limb it covers an area
of 53 feet, seven inches in diameter.
This famous tree is productive in
accordance with its size. Its record
crop in any one season is a little
over 2,000 pounds of cherries.

Louisiana Land Legislation.
Fred J. Grace, register of the state
land office, has received the following
letter from United Stater Senator Mur
phy J. Foster.:
"I am just in receipt of your letter
of the 12th inst., inclosing a copy of Act
No. 212, senate concurrent resolution,
by Mr. McCulloh, memorializing con
gress to have passed at this session
a bill granting to Louisiana the public
lands of the United States situated
therein, the proceeds of the sale of
which to be used for the support of
the common schools, and making pro
vision for the recognition of any an
terior claims so that the same will not
be cotflicted with. In reply, I will
state, in all frankness, that it will be
absolutely impossible to secure the
passage of such a bill at this session.
Many of the committees have already
given notice that they will consider no
new bills, if referred to them, and in
fact every effort is being made to dis
pose of legislation which is now crowd
ing the committee dockets and on the
calendar awaiting passage. I, there
fore, do not think that anything could
be accomplished by attempting to rush
this matter through congress in the
few remaining days of this session,
but will be glad to take the matter up
in the next congress, and will com
municate with you further upon-the
subject.
